The multiverse does not remove the need for God. Even if many universes existed, the whole system would still need explanation.

A multiverse generator would require laws, mathematical structure, causal power, and the ability to produce life-permitting worlds. That simply pushes the question back: why does such a system exist, and why does it have those ordered properties rather than nothing or chaos? Multiplying universes does not explain why there is reality at all or why reality is governed by intelligible order.

The Christian answer reaches deeper than one universe or many universes: all contingent reality depends on the eternal Creator. A multiverse, if it existed, would still be created, sustained, and known by God. And if someone defines a multiverse as including every genuine possibility, that does not weaken classical theism: a necessary God would not be confined to one possible universe, because necessary being is not local or contingent.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.” Genesis 1:1
“From Him and through Him and to Him are all things.” Romans 11:36
“He upholds the universe by the word of His power.” Hebrews 1:3

questions.question: Is Christianity just one truth among many?
questions.answer:
Christianity is not one truth among many equal truths. It makes specific claims about reality: God exists, Jesus is Lord, sin is real, Christ rose from the dead, and salvation is found in Him.

Different religions and worldviews disagree on central questions. God cannot both be triune and not triune in the same sense. Jesus cannot both be the crucified and risen Son of God and merely a prophet whose crucifixion did not happen. Contradictory claims cannot all be true together. Respect for people does not require pretending all beliefs are equally true.

Many religions can be described as human beings reaching toward God, truth, or transcendence. Christianity makes the opposite central claim: God reached down to us in Christ. The Gospel is not first our climb toward God, but God coming to seek and save sinners. Christianity invites honest examination because truth is not created by preference. If Jesus rose from the dead, His claim is not one spiritual option; it is reality to which every person must respond.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I am the way, and the truth, and the life.” John 14:6
“There is salvation in no one else.” Acts 4:12
“The truth will set you free.” John 8:32

questions.question: How do I answer the divine hiddenness argument?
questions.answer:
God is not hidden in the sense that He has left Himself without witness. He reveals Himself through creation, conscience, Scripture, and supremely through Jesus Christ. But He does not force Himself on people in a way that removes the need for trust, humility, and repentance.

Many people want God to be undeniable on their terms while remaining free from His authority. Scripture says the problem is not only lack of information; it is also the human heart. God gives real light, but sinners often suppress, resist, or reinterpret it. At the same time, honest anguish over God’s silence should be treated tenderly, not dismissed. The Bible itself contains prayers from people who feel abandoned.

The Christian answer is that God has made Himself known most clearly in the crucified and risen Christ, who entered our darkness to bring us to God.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“His invisible attributes... have been clearly perceived.” Romans 1:20
“No one has ever seen God; the only God... He has made Him known.” John 1:18
“You will seek Me and find Me, when you seek Me with all your heart.” Jeremiah 29:13

questions.question: Can science answer questions of purpose?
questions.answer:
Science can answer many questions about mechanisms, patterns, and physical causes, but it cannot finally answer questions of ultimate purpose.

Science can describe how the heart pumps blood, how stars form, or how ecosystems function. It cannot tell us why there is a universe, why human beings have dignity, why truth matters, or what life is ultimately for. Those are philosophical and theological questions. Christianity says purpose comes from the Creator: all things exist through Christ and for Christ.

Science is a powerful gift when used rightly, but it becomes too small when treated as the only path to knowledge.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“All things were created through Him and for Him.” Colossians 1:16
“The heavens declare the glory of God.” Psalm 19:1
“Whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God.” 1 Corinthians 10:31

questions.question: Why believe in objective truth?
questions.answer:
Objective truth must exist because even denying it makes a truth claim. The statement “there is no objective truth” is itself being offered as objectively true.

Without objective truth, meaningful conversation collapses. We could not say injustice is really wrong, evidence really matters, or a belief is actually false. Christianity grounds truth in God’s own character. God does not merely prefer truth; He is truthful, faithful, and unable to lie. Because He made the world and made humans in His image, our minds can genuinely know truth, though not exhaustively.

Truth is not oppressive. Lies enslave; truth frees, especially when truth leads us to Christ.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“God is not man, that He should lie.” Numbers 23:19
“I am the way, and the truth, and the life.” John 14:6
“Sanctify them in the truth; Your word is truth.” John 17:17

questions.question: Does neuroscience disprove the soul?
questions.answer:
Neuroscience does not disprove the soul. It shows that the brain and our mental life are deeply connected, which Christianity already expects because human beings are embodied creatures.

Brain injury, chemistry, and development can affect memory, mood, and behavior. That does not prove the person is only a brain. A damaged instrument can affect the music without proving there is no musician. Human consciousness, rationality, moral responsibility, self-awareness, and longing for God point beyond a purely material explanation. Christianity teaches that humans are embodied souls: not ghosts trapped in machines, and not machines pretending to be persons.

The body matters deeply, but the person is more than matter. We are made for resurrection, not disembodied escape.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The LORD God... breathed into his nostrils the breath of life.” Genesis 2:7
“Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul.” Matthew 10:28
“Your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit.” 1 Corinthians 6:19

questions.question: Is belief in God a gaps argument?
questions.answer:
Belief in God is not a gaps argument when God is understood as the Creator and sustainer of everything, not a temporary explanation for what science has not yet discovered.

A gaps argument says, “We do not know how this works, therefore God did it.” Christianity says something deeper: even the things we can explain scientifically still depend on God for their existence, order, and intelligibility. God is not squeezed into unknown gaps; He is the reason there is a universe with laws, minds, beauty, morality, and purpose at all.

Scientific explanations describe created processes. They do not remove the Creator who upholds those processes.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“In Him we live and move and have our being.” Acts 17:28
“He is before all things, and in Him all things hold together.” Colossians 1:17
“The earth is the LORD’s and the fullness thereof.” Psalm 24:1

questions.question: If God is outside time, how can He act in time?
questions.answer:
If God is outside time, He can still act in time because He is not absent from His creation. He transcends time while sustaining every moment of time.

God’s eternity does not mean He is frozen or incapable of relationship. It means He is not limited by past, present, and future the way creatures are. The author of a story is not trapped inside the timeline of the story, but can write events within it. In a far greater way, God can create, speak, judge, answer prayer, and enter history without becoming limited by time.

The clearest example is the incarnation: the eternal Son entered our history without ceasing to be eternal God.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Before the mountains were brought forth... from everlasting to everlasting You are God.” Psalm 90:2
“With the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day.” 2 Peter 3:8
“When the fullness of time had come, God sent forth His Son.” Galatians 4:4

questions.question: When Muslims say memorisation makes the Qur’an more trustworthy than the Bible, how should Christians respond?
questions.answer:
When Muslims say memorisation makes the Qur’an more trustworthy than the Bible, Christians should affirm the value of memorisation while distinguishing memorability from the wider question of textual history. A message can be memorised beautifully and still need to be tested.

Christians are grateful for memorization and oral preservation, but the key questions are different: Does the message agree with earlier revelation? Is it historically grounded? What does it say about Jesus? The Bible’s trustworthiness rests on God’s providence, manuscript evidence, public apostolic testimony, and the fulfillment of God’s promises in Christ. The Christian Scriptures were copied, translated, read publicly, quoted widely, and preserved across communities.

A text should not be judged only by memorability. It should be judged by truth, history, and whether it faithfully reveals God.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Christ died for our sins... He was buried... He was raised.” 1 Corinthians 15:3-4
“Contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints.” Jude 3
“The word of the Lord remains forever.” 1 Peter 1:25

questions.question: When Muslims say Islam is fairer because it has no original sin, how should Christians respond?
questions.answer:
When Muslims say Islam is fairer because it has no original sin, Christians should define original sin carefully and then point to Christ. Christianity gives both a deeper diagnosis of the human problem and a deeper account of mercy.

Original sin means we inherit a fallen condition in Adam: corruption, death, alienation from God, and a heart bent away from Him. It does not mean every person is as evil as possible or that infants commit Adam’s personal act in the same way Adam did. It means the human race is not morally neutral; sin is not merely bad education or bad examples.

The fairness question must also include Christ. Christianity teaches representation in both directions: in Adam, humanity falls; in Christ, God provides a new representative whose obedience, death, and resurrection bring life. Grace is not less fair than self-salvation. It is mercy beyond merit, because God Himself provides the Saviour we could not provide.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin.” Romans 5:12
“By the one man’s obedience the many will be made righteous.” Romans 5:19
“As in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive.” 1 Corinthians 15:22

questions.question: When Muslims say Islam takes fasting more seriously than Christianity, how should Christians respond?
questions.answer:
Christians should first acknowledge the seriousness and public discipline of fasting in Islam. It is not wise to mock a practice that many Muslims undertake with real devotion and sacrifice.

The Christian response is not that fasting is unimportant. Jesus assumes His disciples will fast, but He warns against using fasting as a public display of righteousness. In Christianity, fasting is not a way to earn God’s favor or prove spiritual superiority. It is a bodily act of humility, repentance, prayer, dependence, and hunger for God.

So the issue is not simply which religion has the more visible fasting calendar. The deeper question is what fasting means. Christians fast from grace, not to purchase grace; they fast because the Bridegroom has come, because they await His return, and because they want their bodies to join their prayers.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“When you fast, do not look gloomy like the hypocrites.” Matthew 6:16
“The days will come when the bridegroom is taken away from them, and then they will fast.” Matthew 9:15
“Present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God.” Romans 12:1

questions.question: When Muslims say Islamic law gives a fuller social order than Christianity, how should Christians respond?
questions.answer:
When Muslims say Islamic law gives a fuller social order than Christianity, Christians should not answer as though faith is merely private. Christianity gives a different kind of public vision: every area of life belongs to Christ, but the church is not a coercive state and conversion is not forced by law.

Christianity has strong public implications. Every person has dignity because all are made in God’s image. State authority is real but limited under God. The poor and vulnerable must be protected. Marriage and family matter. Truthfulness, mercy, justice, religious non-coercion, and care for enemies flow from Christ’s lordship. Christians should seek justice in society, but the kingdom is not advanced by forcing outward religious conformity.

Jesus forms a people from every nation who bear witness, practice mercy, and obey Him as Lord. That is not a lack of social concern; it is a kingdom shaped by the cross rather than by compulsion.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“My kingdom is not of this world.” John 18:36
“Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar’s, and to God the things that are God’s.” Mark 12:17
“Do justice, and love kindness, and walk humbly with your God.” Micah 6:8

questions.question: When Muslims suggest the Bible and Qur’an could both be from God, how should Christians respond?
questions.answer:
When Muslims suggest the Bible and Qur’an could both be from God, Christians should honor the desire for peace while explaining that God does not reveal contradictory gospels. The two texts cannot both have come from God in the same sense if they contradict each other on central truths about Jesus and salvation.

The New Testament teaches that Jesus is the eternal Son, was crucified, rose bodily, and saves sinners through His death and resurrection. The Qur’an denies key parts of that message. God does not reveal contradictory gospels. Christians should compare the claims carefully and respectfully, but they cannot treat both as equally true revelations from God.

The question comes down to Jesus. If the apostolic witness is true, then any later message that denies the Son and the cross cannot be from the same divine source.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“God is not man, that He should lie.” Numbers 23:19
“Whoever denies the Son does not have the Father.” 1 John 2:23
“Even if we or an angel from heaven should preach... a gospel contrary... let him be accursed.” Galatians 1:8

questions.question: How can Christians recognise that Muslims love Jesus while explaining the differences?
questions.answer:
Christians can sincerely acknowledge that many Muslims love and honor Jesus as they understand Him. That should be met with respect, not mockery, while still allowing an honest conversation about who Jesus is.

But love for Jesus must be joined to truth about who He is. The New Testament presents Jesus not only as Messiah and prophet, but as the eternal Son who became flesh, died for sins, rose from the dead, and receives worship. If someone honors a version of Jesus who is not crucified, not divine, and not the Son, Christians must gently say this is not the Jesus of the apostles.

A respectful answer can begin with common ground, but it should lead clearly to the biblical Christ.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Who do you say that I am?” Matthew 16:15
“The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.” John 1:14
“God has made Him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified.” Acts 2:36

questions.question: Did Jesus’ promise of the Comforter predict a later prophet?
questions.answer:
Jesus’ promise of the Comforter does not predict a later prophet. In John’s Gospel, the Comforter is the Holy Spirit, whom Jesus sends to dwell with His disciples.

The context is clear. Jesus says the Comforter will be with the disciples, dwell in them, remind them of what Jesus taught, testify about Jesus, and glorify Jesus. This was fulfilled in the coming of the Holy Spirit, especially seen at Pentecost. A later human prophet does not fit the description: the Comforter is already identified as “the Spirit of truth” and “the Holy Spirit,” dwells in Jesus’ disciples, remains with them, reminds them of Jesus’ teaching, and glorifies Christ from within the apostolic witness.

The promise is not that Jesus’ revelation would be corrected later. It is that the Spirit would empower the apostles to bear faithful witness to Him.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He will give you another Helper... even the Spirit of truth.” John 14:16-17
“The Helper, the Holy Spirit... will teach you all things.” John 14:26
“When the Helper comes... He will bear witness about Me.” John 15:26

questions.question: When Muslims say Christian assurance sounds arrogant, how should Christians explain salvation by Christ?
questions.answer:
When Muslims say Christian assurance sounds arrogant, Christians should explain that assurance rests on union with Christ, not on a believer’s confidence that they have done enough. Assurance is humble confidence in Jesus, not presumption about ourselves.

The believer is justified because Christ’s righteousness is counted to them. The believer is adopted because the Father receives them in the Son. The believer is kept because Christ intercedes and holds His sheep. Good works matter, but they are fruit of salvation, not the foundation of peace with God.

This should be spoken respectfully in comparison with Islam. Christianity does not say believers are saved because they are morally impressive. It says Christ is sufficient. Assurance says, “I am not enough, but Jesus is enough; my hope is not in the weight of my deeds, but in the crucified and risen Saviour who keeps His people.”

KEY SCRIPTURE
“There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.” Romans 8:1
“I write these things... that you may know that you have eternal life.” 1 John 5:13
“He always lives to make intercession for them.” Hebrews 7:25

questions.question: Does the Trinity compromise pure monotheism?
questions.answer:
The Trinity does not compromise pure monotheism as Christians define it. Christians confess one God, not three gods, and not one God with partners beside Him.

The question is what kind of oneness God has revealed. Scripture teaches there is one God, yet the Father is God, the Son is God, and the Spirit is God, with real personal distinction between them. This is not adding creatures to God. The Son and Spirit share the one divine being eternally. The Trinity is not a denial of monotheism; it is Christian monotheism as revealed in Christ and Scripture.

The one God is not solitary emptiness. He is eternally Father, Son, and Spirit, and His saving love flows from who He is.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The LORD our God, the LORD is one.” Deuteronomy 6:4
“I and the Father are one.” John 10:30
“The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all.” 2 Corinthians 13:14

questions.question: Why does God seem silent when I ask for guidance?
questions.answer:
God’s silence does not mean He is absent or uncaring, and Christians should not answer as if guidance is always obvious. Scripture contains many faithful people who wait, lament, and ask for light while still trusting God.

Many people want guidance to feel unmistakable, but God often forms maturity by teaching us to walk wisely with what He has already revealed. If the issue involves sin, Scripture is clear. If it involves wisdom, God invites prayer, counsel, and careful decision-making. Silence may also expose impatience, fear, or desire for control. But God is not cruel; He is a Father who leads His people even when they cannot see every step.

The clearest guidance God has given is Christ Himself: follow Him, obey His Word, and trust His care.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path.” Psalm 119:105
“If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God.” James 1:5
“Trust in the LORD with all your heart... and He will make straight your paths.” Proverbs 3:5-6

questions.question: Can God be loving if He commands worship?
questions.answer:
God commands worship because He is supremely worthy, not because He is needy. He is the highest good, and humans are made for communion with Him. Christians should still acknowledge why the command can sound troubling before explaining the difference between divine glory and human ego.

A vain person demands praise they do not deserve. God deserves all worship because He is the Creator, Sustainer, and source of every good thing. If He directed us to worship something else, He would be directing us toward falsehood. Human beings become like what they worship. Worshiping money, pleasure, power, approval, or self deforms the soul. Worshiping God restores us to reality.

God’s call to worship is an invitation back to the purpose for which we were made.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“You shall worship the Lord your God and Him only shall you serve.” Matthew 4:10
“Worthy are You, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power.” Revelation 4:11
“In Your presence there is fullness of joy.” Psalm 16:11

questions.question: How can God be jealous without being petty?
questions.answer:
God’s jealousy is not petty insecurity. In Scripture it is covenant love refusing to treat destructive idols as harmless, though Christians should explain this carefully because human jealousy often looks possessive and sinful.

Human jealousy is often selfish because we envy what is not ours. God’s jealousy is different: worship truly belongs to Him, and giving our hearts to idols is spiritual adultery. A husband’s righteous jealousy for a marriage covenant is not pettiness; it is love protecting what is sacred. In a greater way, God’s jealousy defends His glory and our good.

That is good for us because we become like what we worship. Idols shrink, enslave, and finally destroy the people who trust them. God’s jealousy is His loving refusal to surrender us to false gods that cannot save. He guards His worship because His glory is our life.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The LORD, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God.” Exodus 34:14
“You shall have no other gods before Me.” Exodus 20:3
“Little children, keep yourselves from idols.” 1 John 5:21

questions.question: If God is sovereign, are human choices meaningful?
questions.answer:
God’s sovereignty does not make human choices meaningless. Scripture teaches both that God rules over all things and that human decisions are real, responsible, and morally significant.

The Bible does not treat these truths as enemies. Joseph’s brothers freely sinned against him, yet God sovereignly used their evil for good. The cross is the clearest example: wicked people acted responsibly in crucifying Jesus, and yet God accomplished His saving plan through it. God’s sovereignty is not fatalism. It means human choices unfold within His wise rule, not outside it.

Because God is sovereign, our choices matter within a world governed by purpose rather than chaos.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good.” Genesis 50:20
“This Jesus... delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified.” Acts 2:23
“Work out your own salvation... for it is God who works in you.” Philippians 2:12-13

questions.question: Why does God reveal Himself through a book?
questions.answer:
God reveals Himself through a book because words can preserve, clarify, and publicly communicate His truth across generations and cultures.

God has also revealed Himself through creation, conscience, mighty acts, prophets, and supremely through Jesus Christ. But written Scripture gives a stable public standard that can be copied, translated, checked, taught, memorized, and preserved beyond private impressions. Without Scripture, people could easily confuse God’s voice with rumor, emotion, power, or tradition.

God’s written Word is not a replacement for knowing Him. It is one of His chosen means to bring us to Christ and keep His people anchored in truth.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Write this as a memorial in a book.” Exodus 17:14
“These are written so that you may believe.” John 20:31
“The sacred writings... are able to make you wise for salvation.” 2 Timothy 3:15

questions.question: How can God be both near and transcendent?
questions.answer:
God is transcendent because He is above creation, not limited by it. God is near because He is personally present to sustain, know, hear, and save His people.

These truths are not opposites. God is not a local force inside the universe, as though He were one object among others. Yet He is nearer than our breath: He sustains our life, hears prayer, searches the heart, and comes to us in Christ. If God were only transcendent, we might think of Him as unreachable. If He were only near in a creaturely way, He would not be the holy Creator.

Christians worship a God greater than the universe and near enough to receive the cries of the brokenhearted.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I dwell in the high and holy place, and also with him who is of a contrite and lowly spirit.” Isaiah 57:15
“He is actually not far from each one of us.” Acts 17:27
“The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.” John 1:14

questions.question: Does God have emotions?
questions.answer:
God does not have emotions in a weak, unstable, creaturely way, but He is not cold or impersonal. Scripture truly reveals His love, compassion, anger, grief, delight, and mercy.

Human emotions often control us, change us unpredictably, or arise from ignorance. God is never overwhelmed, manipulated, or surprised. His affections are holy, wise, and consistent with His unchanging character. When Scripture speaks of God’s anger, it reveals His settled opposition to evil. When it speaks of His compassion, it reveals His real mercy toward the needy.

God is not less personal than we are. He is perfectly personal, without our sin, instability, or weakness.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The LORD is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love.” Psalm 103:8
“I the LORD do not change.” Malachi 3:6
“God so loved the world, that He gave His only Son.” John 3:16

questions.question: What does Christianity say to someone who is spiritual but not religious?
questions.answer:
To someone who is spiritual but not religious, Christianity says the longing for something more is real, but spirituality needs truth, not just experience.

Being “spiritual” can mean many things: openness to transcendence, desire for peace, rejection of empty religion, or hunger for meaning. Christianity agrees that lifeless religion cannot save. But it also says we cannot design God from our preferences. The true God has made Himself known in Jesus Christ. We do not need vague spirituality; we need reconciliation with God, forgiveness of sin, and new life.

Jesus does not call people into empty ritual. He calls them to Himself.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“True worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth.” John 4:23
“No one comes to the Father except through Me.” John 14:6
“Come to Me... and I will give you rest.” Matthew 11:28

questions.question: Can someone lose salvation?
questions.answer:
Christians differ on how to explain warnings and perseverance, but a specific Christian answer should say this: no one who truly belongs to Christ will finally be lost, because salvation is kept by Christ’s grace, not by human strength.

At the same time, true faith does not make peace with abandoning Christ. Scripture gives serious warnings against falling away because false faith, hardened sin, and settled rejection of Christ are spiritually deadly. Those warnings are one of God’s means of keeping His people awake and persevering. A Christian who is weak, tempted, or afraid should run to Christ, not assume they have fallen beyond Him. A person who proudly abandons Christ should not be comforted with empty assurance.

Assurance rests in Christ’s grip, and that assurance leads believers to keep trusting, repenting, and following Him.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“No one will snatch them out of My hand.” John 10:28
“The one who endures to the end will be saved.” Matthew 24:13
“He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion.” Philippians 1:6

questions.question: Why do Christians confess sin regularly if already forgiven?
questions.answer:
Christians confess sin regularly not because Christ’s forgiveness is weak, but because fellowship with God is real and sin still matters.

A justified believer is accepted in Christ, but still needs daily cleansing, honesty, and repentance. In a healthy family, a child remains a child even after disobedience, but confession restores openness and trust. Regular confession also kills pride. It keeps Christians from pretending they are beyond grace and brings sins into the light where Christ’s mercy is applied.

Confession is not paying God back. It is returning to the Father who has already opened the way through Jesus.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Forgive us our debts.” Matthew 6:12
“If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us.” 1 John 1:9
“There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.” Romans 8:1

questions.question: Is baptism necessary for salvation?
questions.answer:
Baptism is commanded by Jesus and should not be treated as optional, but baptism does not save as a work that earns forgiveness apart from faith in Christ.

In the New Testament, baptism is the public sign of union with Christ: cleansing, death to the old life, and rising to new life. It belongs with repentance and faith. Christians differ on some details, but the center is clear: the saving power is Christ Himself, received by faith. The thief on the cross was saved without baptism, yet no obedient Christian should despise baptism when it is available.

Baptism does not replace the Gospel. It visibly proclaims the Gospel.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ.” Acts 2:38
“Baptism... now saves you, not as a removal of dirt from the body but as an appeal to God for a good conscience.” 1 Peter 3:21
“Today you will be with Me in paradise.” Luke 23:43

questions.question: What is union with Christ?
questions.answer:
Union with Christ means believers are joined to Jesus by faith through the Holy Spirit, so what is His becomes theirs by grace.

This is one of the deepest truths of salvation. Christians are not merely forgiven from a distance. They are united to Christ in His death and resurrection. His righteousness covers them, His life works in them, His Spirit dwells in them, and His future becomes their future. Union with Christ is why justification, adoption, sanctification, and resurrection hope belong together.

The Gospel is not only that Christ did something for us, but that He gives Himself to us.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me.” Galatians 2:20
“You have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God.” Colossians 3:3
“If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation.” 2 Corinthians 5:17

questions.question: Why does Christianity say I cannot save myself?
questions.answer:
Christianity says you cannot save yourself because the problem is deeper than ignorance, weakness, or a few bad habits. Sin makes us guilty before God and spiritually unable to restore ourselves.

If salvation were simply moral improvement, then effort might be enough. But sinners need forgiveness, a new heart, reconciliation with God, and resurrection life. Even our righteous deeds cannot make us clean before a perfect God; Isaiah says they are like a polluted garment when used as the basis of our standing before Him. No amount of good works can erase guilt already incurred or create spiritual life in a dead heart. That is why Jesus came: not to assist basically healthy people, but to save the lost.

The Gospel humbles us, but it also gives hope. What we cannot do, Christ has done.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“All our righteous deeds are like a polluted garment.” Isaiah 64:6
“Apart from Me you can do nothing.” John 15:5
“Even when we were dead in our trespasses, [God] made us alive together with Christ.” Ephesians 2:5
“He saved us, not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to His own mercy.” Titus 3:5

questions.question: What does adoption mean in salvation?
questions.answer:
Adoption means God does not merely pardon believers like released criminals; He welcomes them as beloved children in His family.

Justification answers the courtroom problem: sinners are declared righteous in Christ. Adoption answers the family problem: former rebels are brought near as sons and daughters. Believers receive the Father’s love, the Spirit’s witness, access in prayer, discipline that comes from love, and an inheritance with Christ. This is not sentimental language; it is a legal and relational reality secured by Jesus.

In Christ, the believer can approach God not as a terrified outsider, but as a child received by the Father.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“You have received the Spirit of adoption as sons, by whom we cry, ‘Abba! Father!’” Romans 8:15
“He predestined us for adoption to Himself as sons through Jesus Christ.” Ephesians 1:5
“See what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God.” 1 John 3:1

questions.question: How do I explain penal substitution without jargon?
questions.answer:
In plain language: Jesus willingly stood in our place and took the judgment our sin deserves so we could be forgiven and brought to God.

“Penal” means sin has a real penalty. “Substitution” means Christ stands in the place of sinners. This is not the Father abusing an unwilling Son. The Father sends, the Son freely gives Himself, and the Spirit applies the work of salvation. At the cross, God does not ignore evil. He deals with it through the self-giving sacrifice of His own Son.

We owed a debt we could not pay. Scripture says no mere man can ransom another person before God, because every sinner already owes God their own life and obedience. Jesus can take our place because He is not merely another guilty man; He is the sinless God-man, truly human to represent us and truly divine so His life has infinite worth. Christ paid the debt with His life and rose again so forgiveness could be given righteously.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He was pierced for our transgressions.” Isaiah 53:5
“The Son of Man came... to give His life as a ransom for many.” Mark 10:45
“Christ also suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous.” 1 Peter 3:18

questions.question: How should Christians respond when personal testimony is more helpful than an argument?
questions.answer:
Personal testimony is helpful when the person needs to see that Christianity is not only an argument but a living encounter with Christ’s grace.

A testimony should not replace the Gospel, but it can show how the Gospel became real in a life. Keep it honest, Christ-centered, and not overly dramatic. The point is not “my story proves Christianity,” but “this is how Christ met me, forgave me, changed me, and gave me hope.” Testimony is especially useful when someone is asking whether faith makes any difference, whether God can forgive, or whether Christians are only repeating theories.

A good testimony opens a window, then points through the window to Jesus.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Come and hear... what He has done for my soul.” Psalm 66:16
“One thing I do know, that though I was blind, now I see.” John 9:25
“We proclaim... what we have seen and heard.” 1 John 1:3

questions.question: In a real conversation, how do I use a Bible verse without proof-texting?
questions.answer:
Using a Bible verse without proof-texting means letting the verse speak in its context rather than using it as a slogan.

Before quoting a verse, know who is speaking, who is being addressed, what the surrounding passage says, and how it fits the whole Bible. A verse can be true and still be misused if ripped from its setting. In conversation, it can help to say, “In context, this passage is saying...” and then explain the point simply. Scripture should be handled with reverence, not as ammunition for winning.

The goal is to bring God’s Word to bear faithfully, so the person hears Scripture’s actual message.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Rightly handling the word of truth.” 2 Timothy 2:15
“The sum of Your word is truth.” Psalm 119:160
“They received the word... examining the Scriptures daily.” Acts 17:11

questions.question: In a real conversation, how do I respond to a rapid list of alleged contradictions?
questions.answer:
When someone gives a rapid list of alleged contradictions, do not try to answer all of them at once. Ask them to choose the strongest one and examine it carefully.

A long list can create the feeling that the Bible is overwhelmed, even if each example has a reasonable answer. Slow the pace. Say, “Those are several different issues. Which one do you think is the clearest contradiction?” Then define what would count as a contradiction and look at the actual passages. Many objections disappear when context, genre, wording, and perspective are handled carefully.

One well-answered objection is usually more useful than ten rushed answers.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“If one gives an answer before he hears, it is his folly and shame.” Proverbs 18:13
“The heart of the righteous ponders how to answer.” Proverbs 15:28
“Rightly handling the word of truth.” 2 Timothy 2:15

questions.question: In a real conversation, how do I avoid jargon when explaining doctrine?
questions.answer:
Avoid jargon by translating doctrine into normal language without watering it down. People do not need simpler truth; they often need clear words.

Instead of saying “penal substitutionary atonement,” say, “Jesus willingly took the judgment sinners deserve so they can be forgiven.” Instead of “justification,” say, “God declares believers right with Him because of Christ.” Instead of “incarnation,” say, “The eternal Son of God became truly human.” Once the idea is understood, the theological word can be introduced if helpful.

Clear speech is an act of love. It helps the person hear the Gospel rather than stumble over church vocabulary.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Unless you utter by the tongue speech that is intelligible, how will anyone know what is said?” 1 Corinthians 14:9
“Let your speech always be gracious.” Colossians 4:6
“Write the vision; make it plain.” Habakkuk 2:2

questions.question: What is a good closing sentence for a conversation?
questions.answer:
A good closing sentence should leave the door open, honor the person, and point back to Christ.

Depending on the conversation, you could say: “I really appreciate you talking honestly with me; I think Jesus is worth looking at closely.” Or, “I do not expect you to settle everything tonight, but I would love for you to read one Gospel and ask who Jesus is.” The goal is not to force an emotional ending. It is to leave them with a clear invitation and a sense that you care about them.

The best closing keeps truth and gentleness together.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Come and you will see.” John 1:39
“Taste and see that the LORD is good.” Psalm 34:8
“Let your speech always be gracious, seasoned with salt.” Colossians 4:6

questions.question: Why are there different literary genres in the Bible?
questions.answer:
The Bible uses different literary genres because God communicates truth through history, poetry, law, wisdom, prophecy, letters, apocalyptic vision, and Gospel narrative.

Genre affects how we read. Poetry uses imagery. Narrative tells what happened without approving every action. Law gives covenant instruction. Proverbs give wisdom principles, not mechanical guarantees. Prophecy includes warning, promise, and symbolic language. Letters address churches with doctrine and application. Reading every genre the same way creates confusion.

God did not give a flat textbook. He gave a rich library that speaks to the whole person: mind, imagination, conscience, memory, worship, and obedience.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets.” Hebrews 1:1
“The unfolding of Your words gives light.” Psalm 119:130
“All Scripture is breathed out by God.” 2 Timothy 3:16

questions.question: Why does Jesus call Himself the bread of life?
questions.answer:
Jesus calls Himself the bread of life because He is the true sustenance of the soul. Physical bread keeps the body alive for a time; Jesus gives eternal life.

In John 6, the crowd wants more miraculous bread after the feeding of the five thousand. Jesus redirects them from temporary provision to Himself. He is greater than the manna in the wilderness because He does not merely give bread from heaven; He is the bread from heaven. To come to Him and believe in Him is to receive the life only God can give.

The image is deeply personal: human beings are hungry for forgiveness, life, meaning, and God. Jesus says that hunger is satisfied in Him.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I am the bread of life; whoever comes to Me shall not hunger.” John 6:35
“The bread that I will give for the life of the world is My flesh.” John 6:51
“Man shall not live by bread alone.” Deuteronomy 8:3

questions.question: If Jesus is the Messiah, why is there still no world peace?
questions.answer:
Christians believe Jesus has inaugurated the messianic kingdom, while Jewish objections rightly press Christians to take the promise of world peace seriously. The Hebrew Scriptures speak of nations streaming to Zion, swords beaten into plowshares, and the knowledge of the LORD covering the earth; Christians should not treat those promises as symbolic afterthoughts.

The Hebrew Scriptures give a pattern of suffering before glory. Isaiah 53 speaks of the servant bearing sin before being exalted. Psalm 110 presents David’s Lord reigning at God’s right hand while His enemies are still being subdued. Daniel 7 shows the Son of Man receiving everlasting dominion. Zechariah 9 presents a humble king who brings salvation and later speaks peace to the nations.

Jesus’ first coming dealt with the root cause of war: sin, guilt, Satan, and alienation from God. His resurrection and ascension began His reign. His return will bring the visible universal peace the prophets describe. The continued presence of war does not disprove His messiahship; it shows we live between the Messiah’s atoning first coming and His final public reign.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He was pierced for our transgressions.” Isaiah 53:5
“The LORD says to my Lord: ‘Sit at My right hand.’” Psalm 110:1
“He shall speak peace to the nations.” Zechariah 9:10

questions.question: If Messiah was supposed to gather Israel, how can Jesus be Messiah when exile continues?
questions.answer:
Christians believe Jesus began the promised restoration by gathering a Jewish remnant and bringing Gentiles to Israel’s Messiah, while the fullness of Israel’s restoration remains in God’s hands. That answer should be given humbly, because Jewish readers are right to ask how the visible promises of restoration are fulfilled.

The prophets connect restoration with forgiveness, the Spirit, covenant renewal, and the nations coming to worship Israel’s God. Jesus gathers disciples from Israel, inaugurates the new covenant, pours out the Spirit, and sends the Gospel to the nations. That is not Israel being erased. Gentiles are grafted into Israel’s covenant blessings through Messiah, and Paul warns Gentile Christians not to be arrogant toward Jewish people.

Christians differ on some end-times details, but the core answer is already/not-yet: Messiah has begun restoration through atonement, Spirit, remnant, and worldwide mission; He will complete God’s promises in the final restoration.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah.” Jeremiah 31:31
“I have other sheep... there will be one flock, one shepherd.” John 10:16
“Do not be arrogant toward the branches.” Romans 11:18

questions.question: If Jesus is Messiah, why did He not rebuild the Temple?
questions.answer:
Christians answer that Jesus did not rebuild the stone Temple because He came as the reality to which the Temple pointed: God’s presence, sacrifice, priesthood, and forgiveness. That does not make the Jewish question trivial; it means Christians are claiming fulfillment in a different mode than many expected.

The Temple was where sacrifice was offered and God’s presence was specially associated with His people. Jesus identifies His own body as the true Temple, offers Himself as the final sacrifice, and opens access to God. After His resurrection, the people of God become a Spirit-filled temple built on Him. This does not dishonor the Temple; it fulfills its purpose.

Christians believe the question is not whether Jesus restored worship by rebuilding a building, but whether He brought the greater access to God the Temple anticipated.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up... He was speaking about the temple of His body.” John 2:19-21
“The veil of the temple was torn in two.” Mark 15:38
“We have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus.” Hebrews 10:19

questions.question: If Messiah is expected to reign, how can Christians call a crucified man Messiah?
questions.answer:
Christians call a crucified man Messiah because they believe the prophets reveal a pattern of suffering before final reign. The claim is not that crucifixion obviously looked messianic at first glance, but that Jesus’ death and resurrection reveal how Messiah bears sin and brings redemption.

Many expected Messiah’s victory to appear immediately as political triumph. Jesus reveals a deeper victory first: defeating sin and death. The Tanakh contains patterns of righteous suffering before exaltation: Joseph rejected before saving his family, David hunted before enthronement, and the servant in Isaiah suffering before being exalted. Jesus rises from the dead and is enthroned, and His final reign will be openly displayed.

The cross is not Messiah failing. It is Messiah winning through sacrifice.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“My God, my God, why have You forsaken Me?” Psalm 22:1
“He was wounded for our transgressions.” Isaiah 53:5
“Behold, your king is coming to you... humble and mounted on a donkey.” Zechariah 9:9

questions.question: Where does the Tanakh leave room for Messiah coming before final peace?
questions.answer:
The Tanakh leaves room for Messiah coming before final peace because it presents both suffering and glory, rejection and reign, atonement and worldwide restoration.

Isaiah speaks of a servant who suffers for sin and later is exalted. Daniel speaks of an anointed one being cut off and later of dominion given in connection with the son of man. Zechariah presents a humble king and also a future reign of peace. These themes make sense if Messiah first deals with sin and later brings final peace.

Christians believe Jesus’ first coming accomplished atonement and launched the kingdom; His return will complete the visible peace promised by the prophets.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“After the sixty-two weeks, an anointed one shall be cut off.” Daniel 9:26
“Out of the anguish of His soul He shall see and be satisfied.” Isaiah 53:11
“His dominion is an everlasting dominion.” Daniel 7:14

questions.question: Does believing in Jesus mean a Jewish person stops being Jewish?
questions.answer:
Believing in Jesus does not mean a Jewish person stops being ethnically Jewish. The first followers of Jesus were Jewish, worshiped the God of Israel, and believed Israel’s hope had been fulfilled in the Messiah.

This question carries real emotional weight. Following Jesus may bring painful relational cost with family or community, and Christians should never pressure, manipulate, or speak as if Jewish identity should be erased. The history of forced conversions, antisemitism, and cultural pressure must be acknowledged honestly. Those sins betray the way of Jesus.

In the New Testament, trusting Jesus is not abandoning Abraham, Moses, David, or the prophets. It is receiving the Messiah to whom they point. Messianic Jews exist today: Jewish people who believe Jesus is the Messiah while continuing to identify as Jewish by ancestry, peoplehood, and often forms of Jewish worship and practice. Christians should speak with humility, patience, and deep respect.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Salvation is from the Jews.” John 4:22
“I myself am an Israelite, a descendant of Abraham.” Romans 11:1
“To them belong the patriarchs, and from their race... is the Christ.” Romans 9:5

questions.question: Why have so many Jewish people rejected Jesus if He is really Messiah?
questions.answer:
Many Jewish people have rejected Jesus, but rejection by many does not by itself decide whether He is Messiah. Christians should say this with humility, because the history of Jewish-Christian relations includes deep wounds and serious failures by professing Christians.

Christians should never speak about Jewish unbelief with arrogance. Paul warns Gentile believers directly against pride. The first followers of Jesus were Jewish, and the New Testament expects a future mercy for Israel. Reasons for rejection include expectations about Messiah, the scandal of the cross, historical suffering at the hands of professing Christians, and spiritual hardness that Scripture says affects both Jews and Gentiles in different ways.

The right Christian posture is grief, humility, love, prayer, and confidence that God keeps His promises. This question should never be used to speak contemptuously about Jewish people.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He came to His own, and His own people did not receive Him.” John 1:11
“Do not be arrogant toward the branches.” Romans 11:18
“The gifts and the calling of God are irrevocable.” Romans 11:29

questions.question: How can Christians worship Jesus without committing idolatry?
questions.answer:
Christians worship Jesus without intending idolatry because they do not worship Him as a creature beside God. They worship Him as the divine Son who shares the identity, glory, and name of the one God of Israel.

If Jesus were merely a man, worshiping Him would be idolatry. The New Testament agrees that worship belongs to God alone. But it presents Jesus doing what only God does: forgiving sin, ruling creation, receiving divine honor, raising the dead, and sharing God’s glory. The earliest Jewish believers did not abandon monotheism; they recognized that the one God had revealed Himself through the Son.

Christian worship of Jesus is not intended as a denial of the God of Israel. It is worship of the God of Israel made known in Messiah, which is why Christians should explain the claim carefully rather than dismiss Jewish concern about idolatry.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“My Lord and my God!” John 20:28
“That all may honor the Son, just as they honor the Father.” John 5:23
“At the name of Jesus every knee should bow.” Philippians 2:10

questions.question: Does the Trinity violate the Shema: “Hear, O Israel, the LORD is one”?
questions.answer:
Christians do not believe the Trinity violates the Shema, because they confess one LORD, not three gods. The disagreement is not over whether God is one, but over whether the one God has revealed personal distinction within His own being.

The Shema denies polytheism and calls Israel to covenant loyalty to the one LORD. Christianity fully affirms that. The New Testament, written by Jewish believers, includes Jesus within the divine identity without adding another god. Paul can echo the Shema while speaking of “one God, the Father” and “one Lord, Jesus Christ.” The Spirit too is God’s own presence, not a creaturely partner.

Christians are not replacing the Shema. They are confessing that the one LORD has revealed Himself as Father, Son, and Spirit.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one.” Deuteronomy 6:4
“For us there is one God, the Father... and one Lord, Jesus Christ.” 1 Corinthians 8:6
“The Lord is the Spirit.” 2 Corinthians 3:17

questions.question: Numbers says God is not a man. How can Christians say God became man?
questions.answer:
Numbers 23:19 says God is not a man in the sense that He is not a lying, changeable creature. Christians should acknowledge the force of the objection, then explain that the verse denies God’s creaturely unreliability, not His ability to take on human nature if He chooses.

The contrast in Numbers is between God’s faithfulness and human unreliability: “God is not man, that He should lie.” Christians fully affirm that. The incarnation does not mean God stopped being God or became a sinful creature. It means the eternal Son took on real humanity while remaining the faithful, unchanging God. The verse denies that God is creaturely and deceitful; it does not forbid God from coming near in mercy.

In Jesus, God’s faithfulness is not weakened. It is displayed: the God who cannot lie keeps His promises by coming to save.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“God is not man, that He should lie.” Numbers 23:19
“The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.” John 1:14
“I the LORD do not change.” Malachi 3:6

questions.question: The Messiah should be a human Davidic king, not divine. Why call Jesus divine?
questions.answer:
Christians agree that Messiah is a real human Davidic king. They also argue that the Tanakh leaves room for this king to share divine identity and authority in a way no ordinary ruler can, though that claim should be made carefully rather than assumed.

This does not mean Christians add a second god beside the God of Israel. Christian faith remains monotheistic. The claim is that the one God of Israel has revealed His Word or Son in a way that shares His divine identity. Psalm 110 places David’s Lord at God’s right hand. Daniel 7 presents a son of man receiving everlasting dominion and worshipful service from all nations. Isaiah 9 calls the promised child “Mighty God.”

Christians believe Jesus fulfills both lines: He is truly David’s son according to His humanity and truly Lord according to His divine identity.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“To us a child is born... Mighty God.” Isaiah 9:6
“The LORD says to my Lord: ‘Sit at My right hand.’” Psalm 110:1
“To Him was given dominion and glory and a kingdom.” Daniel 7:14

questions.question: If God has no body or wife, what do Christians mean by Son of God?
questions.answer:
“Son of God” does not mean God has a body, wife, or physical offspring. Christians reject that idea. The title speaks of Jesus’ relationship with the Father, His divine nature, and His messianic kingship, not biological generation.

In Scripture, “son” can mean sharing nature, representing authority, or standing in covenant relationship. Israel is called God’s son, the Davidic king is called God’s son, and Jesus fulfills these patterns in the highest way. He is not created by physical generation. He is the eternal Son who reveals the Father perfectly and became man for our salvation.

So Christians are not using biological language about God. We are confessing that Jesus uniquely reveals and shares the Father’s glory.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“You are My Son; today I have begotten You.” Psalm 2:7
“The Word was with God, and the Word was God.” John 1:1
“Whoever has seen Me has seen the Father.” John 14:9

questions.question: Is Isaiah 53 about Israel rather than Jesus?
questions.answer:
Christians should acknowledge that many Jewish interpreters read Isaiah 53 corporately in relation to Israel. Christians then argue that the servant language also fits a representative sufferer whose innocent suffering brings blessing to others, and that Jesus uniquely fulfills that pattern.

In Isaiah, “servant” language is used in more than one way. Sometimes it refers to Israel; sometimes it narrows to a faithful servant who restores Israel. Isaiah 53 describes a righteous sufferer who is rejected, bears guilt, is silent before death, and then is exalted. He suffers not for His own sins but for “our” sins. That fits Jesus with striking clarity: rejected, innocent, pierced, killed, and afterward vindicated.

Christians do not read Isaiah 53 as a random proof-text. We see Jesus fulfilling the servant’s mission: bearing sin and bringing many to righteousness.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He was pierced for our transgressions.” Isaiah 53:5
“By His knowledge shall the righteous one, My servant, make many to be accounted righteous.” Isaiah 53:11
“He Himself bore our sins in His body on the tree.” 1 Peter 2:24

questions.question: Psalm 22 says “like a lion,” not “they pierced my hands and feet.” Does that weaken the Christian case?
questions.answer:
Psalm 22 has a real textual debate, and Christians should not pretend otherwise. The Christian case for the crucifixion does not rest on one disputed phrase alone; the whole psalm’s pattern of righteous suffering, mockery, deliverance, and worldwide praise remains important.

Some manuscripts and translations support “they pierced my hands and feet,” while the Masoretic reading is often rendered “like a lion.” Even if someone rejects “pierced,” Psalm 22 still describes a righteous sufferer mocked, surrounded by enemies, with garments divided by lot, crying out to God, and then seeing worldwide worship result. The crucifixion narratives echo these themes clearly.

Christians can acknowledge the textual discussion honestly while still seeing Psalm 22 fulfilled in the suffering and vindication of Jesus.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“My God, my God, why have You forsaken Me?” Psalm 22:1
“They divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.” Psalm 22:18
“They crucified Him and divided His garments among them, casting lots.” Matthew 27:35

questions.question: Isaiah 7:14 says “young woman,” not “virgin.” Did Christians mistranslate it?
questions.answer:
Isaiah 7:14 uses a Hebrew word that can mean a young woman of marriageable age, and the Greek Jewish translation before Christianity rendered it with a word meaning virgin. Christians should be honest about the lexical issue and not pretend the discussion is simpler than it is.

In Isaiah’s immediate setting, the birth functions as a sign connected to God’s presence with His people. Matthew sees a fuller fulfillment in Jesus, whose birth is uniquely “God with us.” The Christian case for the virgin birth does not rest only on a dictionary argument; it rests on Matthew and Luke’s testimony that Jesus was conceived by the Holy Spirit, and on the larger biblical theme of God bringing salvation by His own power.

Isaiah points to Immanuel; the Gospel announces the deepest meaning of that name in Christ.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call His name Immanuel.” Isaiah 7:14
“That which is conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit.” Matthew 1:20
“The Holy Spirit will come upon you... therefore the child to be born will be called holy,the Son of God.” Luke 1:35

questions.question: Daniel 9 is not about Jesus, is it?
questions.answer:
Daniel 9 is a difficult passage, and Christians should not use it as if every detail were simple. Still, many Christians see a striking pattern of an anointed one, judgment, and a timetable that fits the expectation of Messiah before the Temple’s destruction.

The prophecy speaks of seventy weeks connected with ending transgression, atoning for iniquity, bringing righteousness, an anointed figure, and the later destruction of the city and sanctuary. Christians have differed on the exact calculation of the seventy weeks, but the main shape is striking: an anointed one is cut off before the destruction of Jerusalem and the Second Temple. Jesus was crucified before the Roman destruction of Jerusalem and the Temple in AD 70.

The passage also fits the wider biblical pattern: atonement comes through a rejected Messiah, and the old temple order gives way after His sacrifice. Christians should be honest that the chronology is debated, but the connection to Jesus is not arbitrary. The text points to an anointed one whose death stands at the center of covenant crisis and atonement.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“To finish the transgression, to put an end to sin, and to atone for iniquity.” Daniel 9:24
“An anointed one shall be cut off and shall have nothing.” Daniel 9:26
“When you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, then know that its desolation has come near.” Luke 21:20

questions.question: How can Jesus be the Davidic Messiah if He had no biological father from David?
questions.answer:
Christians usually explain Jesus’ Davidic identity through legal descent via Joseph and, in some traditions, through Mary as well. The point should not be overstated: the claim is that the New Testament presents Jesus as heir to David’s throne, not merely as someone with an interesting genealogy.

Joseph was legally Jesus’ father, and Matthew traces Jesus’ royal line through Joseph to David. Luke also connects Jesus to Davidic descent. In ancient Jewish context, legal fatherhood mattered for inheritance, name, and royal status. The virgin birth does not remove Jesus from David’s house; it shows that He is both the promised son of David and the Son given by God’s direct action.

Jesus is not less messianic because His conception is miraculous. He is David’s promised heir by legal right and God’s promised Son by divine power.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David.” Matthew 1:1
“Joseph, son of David, do not fear to take Mary as your wife.” Matthew 1:20
“He will reign over the house of Jacob forever.” Luke 1:33

questions.question: Does Jeremiah 31 say the new covenant is only for Israel, not the church?
questions.answer:
Jeremiah 31 does say the new covenant and Judah, and Christians should not erase that. Christians believe Gentiles are brought into Israel’s covenant blessings through Messiah, not that the Church simply replaces Israel as if the original promise no longer matters.

The new covenant includes forgiveness of sins, God’s law written on the heart, and true knowledge of the Lord. Jesus inaugurates that covenant with His blood, first among Jewish disciples, and then extends its blessings to the nations as the prophets also anticipated. Gentile inclusion does not erase Israel; it fulfills God’s promise that Abraham’s seed would bless all nations.

The new covenant is Jewish in promise, messianic in fulfillment, and graciously opened to the nations through Jesus.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah.” Jeremiah 31:31
“This cup... is the new covenant in My blood.” Luke 22:20
“In you all the families of the earth shall be blessed.” Genesis 12:3

questions.question: Did Jesus abolish the Torah?
questions.answer:
Jesus did not abolish the Torah as though it were false or evil. Christians believe He fulfilled it, bringing its story, promises, sacrifices, priesthood, and covenant direction to their intended goal.

Fulfillment means more than keeping rules. Jesus embodies faithful Israel, obeys God perfectly, reveals the law’s deepest meaning, offers the sacrifice to which the system pointed, and brings the promised new covenant. Christians therefore do not relate to the Torah as if Messiah has not come. The moral truth of God remains, but the covenant administration changes because the fulfillment has arrived.

Jesus does not discard Moses. He brings Moses’ witness to completion.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them.” Matthew 5:17
“Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes.” Romans 10:4
“Everything written about Me in the Law of Moses... must be fulfilled.” Luke 24:44

questions.question: If the Torah is eternal, why do Christians not keep kosher or Sabbath like Jews?
questions.answer:
Christians do not keep kosher and Sabbath in the same way because they believe the Messiah has fulfilled the Torah’s covenantal signs and brought Gentiles into God’s people without requiring full Torah conversion. That answer should be given carefully, because Jewish obedience to Torah should not be mocked or treated as empty ritual.

Kosher laws and Sabbath marked Israel as God’s covenant people and taught holiness, rest, and separation. In Christ, Gentiles are brought near without becoming Jews, and holiness is defined by union with Messiah rather than food laws. Jesus taught that uncleanness comes from the heart rather than food, and Mark explains that He declared all foods clean. The Sabbath points to rest in God, which Christians find ultimately in Christ. This does not mean the Torah was bad; it means its shadow has met its substance.

Christians still honor God’s holiness and need for worship and rest, but they do not live under the Mosaic covenant as if Jesus had not fulfilled it.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Let no one pass judgment on you in questions of food and drink... or a Sabbath. These are a shadow... but the substance belongs to Christ.” Colossians 2:16-17
“Thus He declared all foods clean.” Mark 7:19
“What God has made clean, do not call common.” Acts 10:15
“Come to Me... and I will give you rest.” Matthew 11:28

questions.question: Did Paul turn the Jewish faith of Jesus into a Gentile religion?
questions.answer:
Paul did not turn Jesus’ Jewish faith into a Gentile religion. Christians believe Paul preached Israel’s Messiah to the nations because the prophets promised that Gentiles would come to the God of Israel.

Paul remained Jewish and argued from the Scriptures. His message was that Gentiles are included by faith in Israel’s Messiah without needing to become Jews under the Mosaic law. That was controversial, but it was not a betrayal of the Tanakh. It was the fulfillment of God’s promise to bless the nations through Abraham’s offspring. Paul did not remove Jewish roots; he insisted Gentile believers were grafted into a story that began with Israel.

The Gospel is not less Jewish because Gentiles are welcomed. It is the Jewish Messiah bringing promised mercy to the world.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“In you all the families of the earth shall be blessed.” Genesis 12:3
“I am saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would come to pass.” Acts 26:22
“You, although a wild olive shoot, were grafted in among the others.” Romans 11:17

questions.question: Does Deuteronomy 13 rule out faith in Jesus as a false path?
questions.answer:
Deuteronomy 13 warns Israel against prophets who lead people after other gods. Christians agree with that warning, so the question is whether faith in Jesus is a departure from the God of Israel or the recognition of Israel’s Messiah.

If Jesus were a creature calling people away from the LORD, Deuteronomy 13 would condemn Christian faith. But the New Testament presents Jesus as the Word made flesh, the Davidic Messiah, the servant who bears sin, and the one who brings people to the Father. He worshiped the God of Israel, fulfilled Israel’s Scriptures, and taught supreme love for the LORD.

The question is therefore Jesus’ identity. Christians do not worship a rival deity. They worship the one God of Israel through the Son He has sent.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“You shall walk after the LORD your God and fear Him.” Deuteronomy 13:4
“I and the Father are one.” John 10:30
“No one comes to the Father except through Me.” John 14:6

questions.question: If sacrifices ended with the Temple, why do Christians say blood atonement is necessary?
questions.answer:
Christians say atonement ultimately requires God’s own provision, not merely a ritual mechanism. The end of Temple sacrifices raises a serious Jewish-Christian difference, and Christians should point to Jesus as the once-for-all sacrifice without implying that Jewish prayer and repentance are insincere.

Leviticus says blood makes atonement by the life. The Day of Atonement centered on sacrifice, priesthood, cleansing, and substitution. The prophets condemn sacrifices without repentance, but they do not say atonement is unnecessary. They condemn empty ritual and call for the heart to return to God.

Christians believe the end of the Temple sacrifices raises the question the New Testament answers: God has provided the final sacrifice in Messiah. Jesus does not make repentance meaningless; He makes forgiveness righteous. He is the suffering servant who bears sin and the once-for-all offering to which the sacrificial system pointed.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“It is the blood that makes atonement by the life.” Leviticus 17:11
“I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice.” Hosea 6:6
“He has appeared once for all... to put away sin by the sacrifice of Himself.” Hebrews 9:26

questions.question: Is the death of Jesus a forbidden human sacrifice?
questions.answer:
Christians do not understand Jesus’ death as a forbidden human sacrifice in which others offer an unwilling victim to manipulate God. They understand it as the voluntary self-giving of the incarnate Son, who offers Himself in love for sinners.

No one takes Jesus’ life from Him against His will. He gives Himself. He is not a helpless victim burned to manipulate a deity; He is the Son who enters humanity to bear sin and defeat death. The cross also ends sacrifice rather than continuing violence. It is once for all, never to be repeated.

Human sacrifice destroys the innocent. Christ’s voluntary self-offering saves sinners by divine mercy and ends the sacrificial pattern rather than extending violence.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“You shall not worship the LORD your God in that way.” Deuteronomy 12:31
“I lay down My life... No one takes it from Me.” John 10:17-18
“Christ loved us and gave Himself up for us.” Ephesians 5:2

questions.question: Judaism teaches repentance and good deeds. Why is Jesus needed for forgiveness?
questions.answer:
Repentance and good deeds matter, deeply, and Christians should not imply Judaism is morally shallow. The Christian claim is that forgiveness ultimately rests on God’s gracious atonement, and that Jesus is the Messiah through whom that atonement is fulfilled.

The Tanakh itself shows that forgiveness is costly. Sacrifice, priesthood, covenant mercy, and substitution all teach that sin cannot simply be waved away. Good deeds are good, but they cannot undo past evil or cleanse the heart completely. Jesus brings what repentance points toward: atonement, forgiveness, and a new heart through the new covenant.

The Christian claim is not that repentance is useless. It is that repentance finds its answer in the Messiah who bears sin and brings us back to God.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Create in me a clean heart, O God.” Psalm 51:10
“I will forgive their iniquity, and I will remember their sin no more.” Jeremiah 31:34
“The Son of Man came... to give His life as a ransom for many.” Mark 10:45

questions.question: Do Christians believe in original sin, and is that foreign to Judaism?
questions.answer:
Christians use “original sin” to describe humanity’s solidarity with Adam and the wounded condition into which humans are born. Christians should explain this carefully, because Jewish traditions often frame sin, responsibility, and repentance differently.

This is not saying every person is as evil as possible or personally committed Adam’s exact act. It means Adam’s rebellion brought corruption and death into the human family. The Hebrew Scriptures already show sin as universal and deep: no one is righteous before God, and humans need cleansing of the heart. Christianity connects that diagnosis to Christ as the new Adam, whose obedience brings life.

Original sin is not meant to make people despair or to deny personal responsibility. It explains why we need redemption deeper than self-improvement.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“There is no one who does not sin.” 1 Kings 8:46
“Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity.” Psalm 51:5
“As in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive.” 1 Corinthians 15:22

questions.question: Why do Christians say Jesus is the Passover Lamb?
questions.answer:
Christians call Jesus the Passover Lamb because His death fulfills the pattern of Passover: deliverance through the blood of a spotless victim.

At Passover, Israel was rescued from judgment and brought out of slavery. The lamb’s blood marked the homes of God’s people. Jesus dies at Passover as the sinless one whose blood brings a greater exodus: freedom from sin, judgment, and death. This does not erase the original Passover; it reveals its deepest goal in God’s plan.

The Exodus rescued Israel from Pharaoh. Jesus rescues His people from the deeper bondage of sin and death.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“When I see the blood, I will pass over you.” Exodus 12:13
“Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!” John 1:29
“Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.” 1 Corinthians 5:7

questions.question: Do Christians read Jesus into texts that Jewish interpreters do not read that way?
questions.answer:
Christians should admit that many Jewish interpreters do not read some key passages in the same way Christians do. The responsible answer is not to mock Jewish readings, but to explain why Christians read the Tanakh through the death and resurrection of Jesus as Messiah.

Jesus and the apostles read Israel’s Scriptures as a unified story moving toward Messiah. The earliest Christians making that claim were themselves Jews, worshiping Israel’s God and arguing from Israel’s Scriptures. That does not remove the disagreement with later Jewish interpreters, but it does mean Christian readings did not begin as a Gentile attempt to hijack a foreign book.

The pattern includes direct promises, patterns, offices, sacrifices, kingship, exile, restoration, wisdom, and suffering righteousness. Christian interpretation is not based only on isolated verses but on the conviction that Jesus’ death and resurrection reveal the goal of the whole story.

The question is whether Jesus really is the Messiah. If He is, then the Scriptures that prepared for Him must be read in the light of His fulfillment.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, He interpreted... the things concerning Himself.” Luke 24:27
“Everything written about Me... must be fulfilled.” Luke 24:44
“The testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy.” Revelation 19:10

questions.question: Is the New Testament anti-Jewish?
questions.answer:
The New Testament is not anti-Jewish in its own setting: Jesus, the apostles, and the earliest believers were Jewish, and many of the disputes are intra-Jewish debates about Israel’s Messiah. Christians must also admit that some texts have been misused in anti-Jewish ways with devastating consequences.

It does contain sharp conflicts, but those are largely intra-Jewish disputes about Jesus, similar to the prophets’ own rebukes of Israel. Christians have sinfully used the New Testament to justify antisemitism, and that must be condemned. The New Testament itself forbids Gentile arrogance, honors Israel’s calling, and teaches that salvation comes from the Jews.

Faithful Christianity rejects antisemitism because it worships the Jewish Messiah, depends on promises God gave through Israel, and forbids Gentile arrogance toward the Jewish people.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Salvation is from the Jews.” John 4:22
“Do not be arrogant toward the branches.” Romans 11:18
“The gifts and the calling of God are irrevocable.” Romans 11:29

questions.question: Why should I consider Jesus after the Crusades, expulsions, and persecution of Jews by Christians?
questions.answer:
Crusades, expulsions, forced conversions, and persecution of Jews by professing Christians must be condemned without evasion. A Jewish person is right to hear Christian claims through that history, and Christians should begin with repentance, humility, and patience before asking anyone to consider Jesus.

A Jewish person has every reason to feel the weight of that history. Christians should begin with repentance and honesty, not excuses. But the sins of those who claimed Christ do not define Christ Himself. Jesus was Jewish, His apostles were Jewish, and He commanded love of neighbor and enemy. Coercion and persecution betray Him. The question worth considering is whether Jesus is true, not whether Christians have always obeyed Him.

The wounds of history require humility. Christians should point to Jesus with tears, not triumphalism, and should never minimize the harm done to Jewish communities in Christ’s name.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Love your neighbor as yourself.” Leviticus 19:18
“Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do.” Luke 23:34
“By this all people will know that you are My disciples, if you have love for one another.” John 13:35

questions.question: Do Jewish people need a mediator, or can they pray directly to God?
questions.answer:
Jewish people can and do pray directly to God, and Christians should not deny the seriousness of Jewish prayer. The Christian claim is that Jesus is the promised mediator who brings final access to the Father, not that Jewish prayer is fake or meaningless.

The Tanakh itself includes priests, sacrifices, intercession, and covenant mediation. Moses intercedes. The priesthood represents the people. Sacrifices deal with guilt. Christians believe these realities point to the final mediator, Jesus the Messiah, who brings people into God’s presence by His atoning death and risen intercession.

Jesus does not block access to God. He provides the access sinners need.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I stood between the LORD and you at that time.” Deuteronomy 5:5
“He bore the sin of many, and makes intercession for the transgressors.” Isaiah 53:12
“There is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.” 1 Timothy 2:5

questions.question: Why trust the Gospels if they were written by Jesus’ followers?
questions.answer:
The Gospels should not be dismissed of Jesus or from apostolic testimony, so they are committed witnesses rather than detached modern reporters. Christians can be honest about that while also arguing that committed testimony can still be historically serious and truthful.

All historical sources have perspectives. The question is whether they are early, connected to eyewitnesses, coherent, and willing to include difficult details. The Gospels name people and places, preserve embarrassing failures of the disciples, and proclaim Jesus’ death and resurrection publicly in the world where the events happened. The apostles did not gain comfort or status by this testimony; many suffered for it.

The Gospel writers were not detached modern journalists, but they were serious witnesses. Their faith came from what they believed God had done in Jesus, and their writings invite readers to examine that claim.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and ministers of the word.” Luke 1:2
“He who saw it has borne witness,his testimony is true.” John 19:35
“We cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard.” Acts 4:20

questions.question: Why should a Jewish person consider the New Testament at all?
questions.answer:
A Jewish person should consider the New Testament because it claims that Israel’s Messiah has come, not because Jewish questions or history should be brushed aside. Christians should invite examination with humility, patience, and respect for the weight of the decision.

The New Testament is not a foreign book detached from the Tanakh. It constantly quotes, echoes, and argues from Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms. It begins with Abraham and David, centers on covenant, sacrifice, kingdom, exile, restoration, and the hope of the nations worshiping Israel’s God.
