Jesus appeared in Jerusalem before the disciples later met Him in Galilee. The Gospels emphasize different resurrection appearances, not competing locations for the first appearance to all believers.

Luke focuses on Jerusalem because his Gospel leads into Acts, where the mission begins in Jerusalem. Matthew highlights Galilee because Jesus had promised to meet the disciples there and because Galilee becomes the setting for the Great Commission. John records appearances in Jerusalem and Galilee. The resurrection appearances occurred over a period of forty days, so multiple locations are expected.

The accounts do not require one single appearance. They present a risen Christ who appeared repeatedly, taught His disciples, and commissioned them.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Jesus Himself stood among them.” Luke 24:36
“The eleven disciples went to Galilee, to the mountain to which Jesus had directed them.” Matthew 28:16
“He presented Himself alive... during forty days.” Acts 1:3

questions.question: Did the disciples go to Galilee immediately or stay in Jerusalem?
questions.answer:
The disciples did not need to go to Galilee immediately in order for Jesus’ words to be true. They remained in Jerusalem at first, then later went to Galilee as instructed.

Luke emphasizes Jerusalem because the ascension and Pentecost mission begin there. Matthew emphasizes the promised Galilee meeting. John shows that the disciples were in Jerusalem on resurrection evening and later in Galilee by the Sea of Tiberias. The resurrection period lasted forty days, allowing both Jerusalem and Galilee appearances.

There is no contradiction in the disciples staying briefly in Jerusalem, meeting Jesus there, and later obeying the Galilee instruction.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Tell My brothers to go to Galilee, and there they will see Me.” Matthew 28:10
“Stay in the city until you are clothed with power from on high.” Luke 24:49
“He presented Himself alive... during forty days.” Acts 1:3

questions.question: Did Jesus ascend on Easter evening or forty days later?
questions.answer:
Jesus ascended forty days after the resurrection. Luke’s Gospel gives a compressed ending, while Acts, written by the same author, gives the fuller timeline.

Ancient writers often summarized events at the end of a book without spelling out every interval. Luke ends his Gospel by moving quickly from resurrection appearances to blessing and ascension. Acts opens by clarifying that Jesus appeared to the disciples over forty days before ascending. Since Luke wrote both works, Acts explains how to read Luke’s compressed conclusion.

The point is not that Jesus ascended twice or that Luke contradicted himself. The risen Lord taught His disciples, then ascended and sent them to wait for the Spirit.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He parted from them and was carried up into heaven.” Luke 24:51
“He presented Himself alive... during forty days.” Acts 1:3
“He was lifted up, and a cloud took Him out of their sight.” Acts 1:9

questions.question: Did Jesus say to baptize or did Paul say he was not sent to baptize?
questions.answer:
Jesus commanded the church to baptize disciples, and Paul’s statement means his personal apostolic priority was preaching the Gospel rather than personally performing most baptisms.

Paul did baptize some believers, so he was not rejecting baptism. His concern in 1 Corinthians was that people were forming factions around human leaders. Paul was grateful he had not baptized many there because no one could claim they were baptized into Paul’s name. Baptism remains commanded by Jesus, but the saving message is Christ crucified, not attachment to the human minister who performs the baptism.

There is no contradiction: baptism is part of discipleship, while the Gospel itself centers on Christ’s death and resurrection.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.” Matthew 28:19
“Christ did not send me to baptize but to preach the gospel.” 1 Corinthians 1:17
“I did baptize also the household of Stephanas.” 1 Corinthians 1:16

questions.question: Did Jesus' last words differ across the Gospels?
questions.answer:
The Gospels preserve different sayings from Jesus on the cross because He spoke more than one sentence while dying. Different last words can be reported depending on which final cry a Gospel writer emphasizes.

Crucifixion lasted hours. Jesus prayed for forgiveness, spoke to the thief, cared for His mother, cried out from Psalm 22, said “I thirst,” declared “It is finished,” and entrusted His spirit to the Father. John emphasizes completion of the saving work. Luke emphasizes trust in the Father. These are not competing final messages; they are complementary windows into the same death.

Together they show Jesus suffering Scripture-fulfilling agony, finishing redemption, and dying in perfect trust.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“It is finished.” John 19:30
“Father, into Your hands I commit My spirit!” Luke 23:46
“My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?” Matthew 27:46

questions.question: Did Jesus drink vinegar or refuse wine?
questions.answer:
Jesus refused drugged wine before crucifixion but later received sour wine while on the cross. These were different drinks offered at different moments for different purposes.

Before the crucifixion, wine mixed with myrrh or gall functioned as a narcotic to dull pain. Jesus refused it, facing the cross consciously. Later, sour wine was offered in response to His thirst and in fulfillment of Scripture. Receiving sour wine did not contradict refusing the earlier drugged drink.

Jesus did not avoid the suffering of the cross. He endured it fully, and even His thirst fulfilled the Scriptures.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“They offered Him wine mixed with myrrh, but He did not take it.” Mark 15:23
“They put a sponge full of the sour wine... and held it to His mouth.” John 19:29
“For My thirst they gave Me sour wine to drink.” Psalm 69:21

questions.question: If Jesus died on Friday and rose on Sunday, how was He in the tomb three days and three nights?
questions.answer:
“Three days and three nights” is best understood as a Jewish idiom for a period spanning parts of three days, not a demand for exactly seventy-two hours in the tomb. The darkness at the crucifixion shows the day was extraordinary, but it is not the strongest reason to count an extra literal night on Friday; inclusive reckoning explains the language more directly.

In biblical time reckoning, any part of a day could be counted as a day. Esther gives a helpful example: she asks the Jews to fast for three days, night or day, and then she acts “on the third day.” That shows idiomatic inclusive reckoning rather than requiring a full seventy-two hours. Jesus died Friday, remained in the tomb through the Sabbath, and rose on the first day of the week: Friday, Saturday, Sunday.

There is no contradiction. Jesus was buried, remained under death, and rose on the third day as He said.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Do not eat or drink for three days, night or day.” Esther 4:16
“On the third day Esther put on her royal robes.” Esther 5:1
“He was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures.” 1 Corinthians 15:4

questions.question: Did Jesus rise after three days or on the third day?
questions.answer:
“After three days” and “on the third day” are compatible Jewish ways of speaking about the same time period. They do not require different resurrection days or a full seventy-two hours.

The Gospels themselves use both kinds of language for the same prediction. If Jesus dies on Friday and rises on Sunday, He rises on the third day by inclusive reckoning: Friday, Saturday, Sunday. “After three days” can mean after the period has begun and reached the third day, not necessarily after seventy-two full hours.

The early Christian confession is consistent: Jesus died, was buried, and rose on the third day.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“After three days He will rise.” Mark 8:31
“On the third day be raised.” Matthew 16:21
“Christ died... He was buried... He was raised on the third day.” 1 Corinthians 15:3-4

questions.question: Did Jesus say He would rebuild the temple or did others misquote Him?
questions.answer:
Jesus truly spoke of rebuilding the temple, but His accusers distorted His meaning. He was speaking about the temple of His body, not threatening to destroy Herod’s temple by violence.

In John, Jesus says, “Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.” John then explains that He meant His body. At His trial, false witnesses twist the saying into an accusation about destroying the physical temple. The witnesses are false not because Jesus never said anything about the temple, but because they misrepresented His claim and intent.

Jesus’ resurrection is the true rebuilding: His crucified body is raised, and access to God is now centered in Him.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.” John 2:19
“He was speaking about the temple of His body.” John 2:21
“We heard Him say, ‘I will destroy this temple.’” Mark 14:58

questions.question: Did Jesus' brothers believe in Him or not?
questions.answer:
Jesus’ brothers did not believe in Him during His earthly ministry, but at least some believed after the resurrection. That is development, not contradiction.

John says His brothers did not believe in Him. Later, Acts shows His brothers gathered with the disciples after the resurrection, and James becomes a leader in the Jerusalem church. Paul also lists an appearance of the risen Jesus to James. The resurrection explains the change from skepticism to faith.

This is powerful evidence that Jesus’ resurrection transformed even members of His own family who had previously doubted Him.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Not even His brothers believed in Him.” John 7:5
“All these with one accord were devoting themselves to prayer... with His brothers.” Acts 1:14
“Then He appeared to James.” 1 Corinthians 15:7

questions.question: Did Paul contradict James on faith and works?
questions.answer:
Paul and James do not contradict each other on faith and works. Paul denies that works can earn justification before God; James denies that dead, fruitless “faith” is saving faith.

Paul is arguing against relying on works of the law as the basis of being declared righteous. James is arguing against empty profession that claims faith while producing no obedience. They use “justify” with different emphases: Paul focuses on God’s declaring sinners righteous through faith in Christ; James focuses on faith being shown to be real by works.

True faith receives Christ apart from works as merit, and then produces works as evidence of living union with Christ.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“A person is justified by faith apart from works of the law.” Romans 3:28
“Faith by itself, if it does not have works, is dead.” James 2:17
“We are His workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works.” Ephesians 2:10

questions.question: Does Paul contradict Jesus about divorce?
questions.answer:
Paul does not contradict Jesus about divorce. He applies Jesus’ teaching to additional situations faced by the church.

Jesus teaches that marriage is God’s covenant design and condemns unlawful divorce. Paul repeats the Lord’s command that husband and wife should not separate. Then Paul addresses a situation Jesus did not directly discuss in the Gospels: abandonment by an unbelieving spouse. Paul’s pastoral instruction does not weaken Jesus’ teaching; it applies the Lord’s moral vision to a mixed-belief household.

The consistent teaching is that marriage is sacred, divorce is grievous, and believers must pursue faithfulness, peace, and obedience to God.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“What therefore God has joined together, let not man separate.” Matthew 19:6
“The wife should not separate from her husband.” 1 Corinthians 7:10
“If the unbelieving partner separates, let it be so.” 1 Corinthians 7:15

questions.question: Does Paul contradict Jesus about food sacrificed to idols?
questions.answer:
Paul does not contradict Jesus about food sacrificed to idols. Jesus teaches that food itself does not defile the heart; Paul warns against participating in idolatry and wounding conscience.

There are two different issues. Food as food is not spiritually defiling in itself. But eating in an idol temple as part of idol worship is fellowship with demons. Even in ordinary eating, love may require limiting freedom if it harms a weaker believer’s conscience. Paul is applying Jesus’ heart-focused ethic to real church situations.

The Christian principle is clear: food is not lord; Christ is. Freedom must be governed by worship and love.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Thus He declared all foods clean.” Mark 7:19
“Flee from idolatry.” 1 Corinthians 10:14
“If food makes my brother stumble, I will never eat meat.” 1 Corinthians 8:13

questions.question: Does Paul contradict the apostles by saying circumcision is nothing?
questions.answer:
Paul does not contradict the apostles by saying circumcision is nothing. He means circumcision is not required for Gentiles to be saved or included in God’s people under the new covenant.

The Jerusalem apostles agreed that Gentile believers should not be forced to be circumcised. Circumcision mattered under the Abrahamic and Mosaic covenant setting, but in Christ the boundary marker of covenant membership is no longer circumcision; it is faith working through love and new creation by the Spirit. Paul even circumcised Timothy for missionary reasons, showing he was not attacking Jewish identity as such.

The issue was salvation. Paul refused to let circumcision become a requirement added to Christ.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Neither circumcision counts for anything nor uncircumcision, but a new creation.” Galatians 6:15
“We should not trouble those of the Gentiles who turn to God.” Acts 15:19
“In Christ Jesus neither circumcision nor uncircumcision counts for anything, but only faith working through love.” Galatians 5:6

questions.question: Was Paul accepted by the Jerusalem apostles immediately or only later?
questions.answer:
Paul was not fully accepted by everyone in Jerusalem immediately, but the apostles later recognized his calling and Gospel. The accounts describe stages in that process.

After Paul’s conversion, many believers feared him because he had persecuted the church. Barnabas vouched for him. Later, Paul met with the recognized leaders and they extended fellowship, acknowledging his mission to the Gentiles. There is no contradiction between initial caution from the wider church and later formal recognition by the apostles.

The story shows both wisdom and grace: the church tested Paul carefully, then received the former persecutor whom Christ had transformed.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“They were all afraid of him, for they did not believe that he was a disciple.” Acts 9:26
“Barnabas took him and brought him to the apostles.” Acts 9:27
“They gave the right hand of fellowship to Barnabas and me.” Galatians 2:9

questions.question: Did Paul's companions hear the voice on the Damascus road or not?
questions.answer:
Paul’s companions heard a sound but did not understand the voice speaking to Paul. The difference is between hearing noise and comprehending the message.

Acts 9 says the men heard the voice or sound but saw no one. Acts 22 says they did not hear the voice of the one speaking to Paul, meaning they did not understand the words addressed to him. This distinction is common even today: someone may hear a voice in the next room without hearing what was said.

The accounts agree that the revelation was directed to Paul, while his companions experienced enough to know something extraordinary had happened.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The men... hearing the voice but seeing no one.” Acts 9:7
“They... did not understand the voice of the one who was speaking to me.” Acts 22:9
“Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?” Acts 9:4

questions.question: Did Paul's companions fall down or stand speechless?
questions.answer:
Paul’s companions fell to the ground at one point and later stood speechless. The accounts describe different moments or aspects of the same event.

Acts 26 says those traveling with Paul fell to the ground when the heavenly light shone around them. Acts 9 says the men stood speechless, hearing the sound but seeing no one. A person can fall under shock and later stand speechless, or “stood” can describe being immobilized in astonishment. The focus in every account is Paul’s encounter with the risen Jesus.

There is no meaningful contradiction. The companions were overwhelmed witnesses to a revelation aimed specifically at Paul.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The men who were traveling with him stood speechless.” Acts 9:7
“When we had all fallen to the ground.” Acts 26:14
“I have appeared to you for this purpose.” Acts 26:16

questions.question: Does Romans say government is good while Revelation portrays empire as evil?
questions.answer:
Romans and Revelation do not contradict each other about government. Romans describes the proper role of governing authority under God; Revelation exposes empire when it becomes idolatrous, violent, and opposed to God.

Government is meant to restrain evil and promote order. Christians should not be anarchists by default. But when political power demands worship, persecutes the saints, or becomes beastly in rebellion against God, Christians must resist its idolatry. The same state can be an instrument of order in one respect and a beastly power when it claims ultimate allegiance.

Christianity neither worships the state nor despises lawful order. Christ alone is Lord over all authorities.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“There is no authority except from God.” Romans 13:1
“It was allowed to make war on the saints.” Revelation 13:7
“We must obey God rather than men.” Acts 5:29

questions.question: Does the Bible say all have sinned while also calling some people blameless?
questions.answer:
When the Bible calls some people blameless, it does not mean they are sinless. It means they are faithful, upright, and sincere in their covenant walk before God.

Scripture is clear that all have sinned and need grace. Yet it can call Job, Zechariah, Elizabeth, or others blameless because their lives are marked by integrity rather than hypocrisy or open rebellion. “Blameless” describes character and public faithfulness, not absolute moral perfection. Only Christ is sinless in the fullest sense.

There is no contradiction between saying believers can be genuinely righteous by grace and saying all humans need forgiveness.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.” Romans 3:23
“That man was blameless and upright.” Job 1:1
“If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves.” 1 John 1:8

questions.question: Does 1 John say Christians do not sin, while also saying Christians do sin?
questions.answer:
First John distinguishes between a believer committing sins and a believer living in settled, unrepentant practice of sin. Christians still sin, but sin no longer defines their new life in Christ.

John says believers must confess sins and that anyone claiming sinlessness is deceived. He also says those born of God do not keep on sinning as a pattern of rebellion. The new birth breaks sin’s dominion, even though believers still struggle and need ongoing cleansing. The difference is between falling into sin and making peace with sin.

The Christian life is not sinless perfection now, but real transformation, repentance, and hope in Christ’s advocacy.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves.” 1 John 1:8
“No one born of God makes a practice of sinning.” 1 John 3:9
“If anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous.” 1 John 2:1

questions.question: Does the Bible say the law is good and also that believers are not under the law?
questions.answer:
The law is good because it comes from God and reveals His righteous will. Believers are not under the law as a covenant system for condemnation or as the way to be justified.

Paul can say the law is holy and good while also saying Christians are not under the law. The law exposes sin, teaches God’s holiness, and points to Christ. But it cannot give sinners the power to obey or make them righteous before God. In Christ, believers live under grace and walk by the Spirit, fulfilling the law’s moral aim through love.

The problem is not the law’s goodness. The problem is human sin and treating the law as a ladder to salvation.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The law is holy, and the commandment is holy and righteous and good.” Romans 7:12
“You are not under law but under grace.” Romans 6:14
“The whole law is fulfilled in one word: ‘You shall love your neighbor as yourself.’” Galatians 5:14

questions.question: Does Jesus save by grace while Revelation judges according to works?
questions.answer:
Jesus saves by grace, and judgment according to works shows the reality of a person’s allegiance. Works are evidence, not the purchase price of salvation.

The Bible never teaches that sinners earn eternal life by balancing enough good deeds against bad ones. Salvation is by grace through faith in Christ. But true faith is not barren. At the final judgment, works reveal whether someone’s life was united to Christ or remained in rebellion. Revelation’s judgment according to works shows God’s justice and exposes false profession.

Grace saves the root; works show the fruit. The fruit does not replace Christ, but it reveals whether faith is living.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“By grace you have been saved through faith... not a result of works.” Ephesians 2:8-9
“The dead were judged... according to what they had done.” Revelation 20:12
“Faith by itself, if it does not have works, is dead.” James 2:17

questions.question: Does the Bible teach predestination and also human choice?
questions.answer:
The Bible teaches both predestination and meaningful human choice. God’s sovereign purpose does not make human decisions fake; it establishes history under His wise rule.

Scripture does not resolve this by denying one side. God chooses, calls, and saves by grace. People are also commanded to repent, believe, obey, and are held responsible for rejecting God. The cross shows both together: Jesus was delivered according to God’s definite plan, and the people who crucified Him were guilty for their actions.

Predestination should produce humility and assurance, not fatalism. Human choice should produce responsibility, not pride.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world.” Ephesians 1:4
“Repent and believe in the gospel.” Mark 1:15
“This Jesus... delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified.” Acts 2:23

questions.question: Does God want all to be saved while not all are saved?
questions.answer:
God genuinely commands all people to repent and takes no pleasure in wickedness, yet not all are saved because many persist in guilty unbelief and God’s providential plan permits rebellion for wise purposes.

The Bible speaks of God’s revealed will: what He commands and delights in morally. He commands repentance, truth, mercy, and faith. It also speaks of His sovereign rule over history, in which He permits human evil without becoming its author. The clearest example is the cross: wicked people freely sinned, yet God accomplished salvation through what they did.

This should lead to urgency, not speculation. The Gospel invitation is sincere: everyone who comes to Christ will be received. If someone is lost, it is not because they wanted mercy and Christ refused them; it is because sinners reject the light and need grace. God’s mercy is real, human guilt is real, and the call to come to Christ is real.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.” 2 Peter 3:9
“You refuse to come to Me that you may have life.” John 5:40
“Whoever comes to Me I will never cast out.” John 6:37

questions.question: Does the Bible say faith is a gift and also command people to believe?
questions.answer:
Faith can be both God’s gift and a real command to people. God’s grace enables what He commands, and people remain responsible to respond.

The Bible commands sinners to believe because Christ is worthy and the Gospel is true. It also teaches that faith is not self-generated spiritual greatness; it is given by grace. This protects both responsibility and humility. No one can say, “I believed because I was better,” and no one can say, “God’s gift makes unbelief innocent.”

God’s command exposes our need; God’s gift supplies what we lack. Salvation is therefore entirely by grace.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved.” Acts 16:31
“By grace you have been saved through faith... it is the gift of God.” Ephesians 2:8
“To you it has been granted... to believe in Him.” Philippians 1:29

questions.question: Does the Bible say God does not remember sins, yet God knows everything?
questions.answer:
When God says He will not remember sins, it does not mean He loses information. It means He will no longer hold those sins against His people in judgment.

God is omniscient; He never becomes ignorant. “Remember” in covenant language often means to act on something. When God remembers mercy, He acts to save. When He does not remember sin, He chooses not to treat the forgiven person according to that guilt. The promise is legal and relational, not a denial of God’s knowledge.

Forgiven sin is not hidden from God’s mind; it is removed from the believer’s account because Christ has borne it.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I will remember their sin no more.” Jeremiah 31:34
“You know when I sit down and when I rise up.” Psalm 139:2
“As far as the east is from the west, so far does He remove our transgressions from us.” Psalm 103:12

questions.question: How do Christians explain the Bible’s teaching on whether the earth lasts forever?
questions.answer:
The Bible can say the earth lasts forever and also speak of the present heavens and earth passing away because Scripture distinguishes God’s enduring creation purpose from the present fallen order.

Ecclesiastes observes the stability of the earth across human generations. Other passages speak of cosmic judgment and renewal. The Christian hope is not that God abandons creation, but that He purifies and renews it. “Passing away” refers to the present corrupted order being judged, not God failing to preserve His creation purpose.

The final hope is new heavens and new earth: continuity with God’s creation, transformed by His power.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The earth remains forever.” Ecclesiastes 1:4
“The heavens will pass away with a roar.” 2 Peter 3:10
“According to His promise we are waiting for new heavens and a new earth.” 2 Peter 3:13

questions.question: Does the Bible say answer softly but Jesus spoke sharply to Pharisees?
questions.answer:
Proverbs teaches that a soft answer often turns away wrath, but it does not mean every faithful word must sound gentle in the same way. Jesus spoke sharply when exposing hardened hypocrisy that harmed others.

A soft answer is wise when it calms anger and prevents needless escalation. But there are moments when direct rebuke is loving and necessary. Jesus’ sharp words against the Pharisees were not emotional loss of control; they were prophetic judgment against leaders who shut people out of the kingdom and burdened the vulnerable. His severity served truth and rescue.

The issue is not soft tone versus firm tone. The issue is whether the words are governed by love, truth, holiness, and the good of the hearers.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“A soft answer turns away wrath.” Proverbs 15:1
“Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites!” Matthew 23:13
“Faithful are the wounds of a friend.” Proverbs 27:6

questions.question: How do Christians explain Jesus calling people fools after warning against saying 'you fool'?
questions.answer:
Jesus warns against contemptuous insult, not every use of the word “fool” in moral discernment. He condemns hatred that dehumanizes another person.

In Matthew 5, Jesus addresses anger that treats someone as worthless and exposes the heart behind murder. Elsewhere, Jesus and Scripture use strong moral language to describe real folly. The difference is not the syllables but the heart and truthfulness of the judgment. Jesus can rightly expose spiritual blindness without sinful contempt. Humans must be careful because our anger is often mixed with pride.

Christians should never use truth as an excuse to despise people made in God’s image.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Whoever says, ‘You fool!’ will be liable to the hell of fire.” Matthew 5:22
“You fools! Did not He who made the outside make the inside also?” Luke 11:40
“The fool says in his heart, ‘There is no God.’” Psalm 14:1

questions.question: Does the Bible say be angry and also forbid anger?
questions.answer:
The Bible permits righteous anger but forbids sinful anger. Anger is sinful when it is selfish, uncontrolled, vengeful, proud, or cherished.

God is rightly angry at evil because He is holy. Jesus showed righteous anger against corruption and hypocrisy. But human anger is dangerous because it often arises from wounded pride or desire for control. That is why Scripture says, “Be angry and do not sin,” while also warning that human anger does not produce God’s righteousness.

Christian anger must be slow, truthful, brief, and surrendered to God. It should seek justice and restoration, not revenge or self-exaltation.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Be angry and do not sin.” Ephesians 4:26
“The anger of man does not produce the righteousness of God.” James 1:20
“Let all bitterness and wrath and anger... be put away from you.” Ephesians 4:31

questions.question: How do Christians explain the Bible’s teaching on wealth being a blessing or a danger?
questions.answer:
Wealth can be a blessing from God, but it is also spiritually dangerous because it easily becomes an idol. The Bible teaches both truths.

In Scripture, God sometimes blesses people materially, and wealth can be used for generosity, justice, hospitality, and worship. But wealth can also create pride, false security, greed, exploitation, and spiritual blindness. Jesus warns the rich strongly because money can make people feel self-sufficient and resistant to grace. The issue is not that every rich person is evil, but that wealth is a powerful rival master.

Christians should receive provision with gratitude, hold it loosely, and use it for God’s glory and neighbor’s good.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The blessing of the LORD makes rich.” Proverbs 10:22
“You cannot serve God and money.” Matthew 6:24
“As for the rich... they are to do good, to be rich in good works.” 1 Timothy 6:17-18

questions.question: Does the Bible say God is love and also that God judges?
questions.answer:
God’s love and God’s judgment are not contradictions. Because God is love, He judges evil that destroys His creatures and dishonors His holiness.

A love that never opposes evil is not moral love. If God ignored abuse, murder, oppression, deception, and rebellion, He would not be good. Judgment means God takes reality seriously. The cross shows love and judgment together: God judges sin in Christ so mercy can be given righteously to sinners who trust Him.

God’s love is not sentimental permission. It is holy love that rescues, purifies, and sets all things right.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“God is love.” 1 John 4:8
“Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just?” Genesis 18:25
“So that He might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus.” Romans 3:26

questions.question: How do Christians explain Jesus’ mercy alongside speaking about hell?
questions.answer:
Jesus’ mercy and His warnings about hell belong together. He warns about hell because He is merciful and does not want sinners to perish.

Mercy does not mean hiding danger. A doctor who refuses to tell a patient the truth is not loving. Jesus speaks more seriously about judgment than many people expect because He sees sin clearly and knows the eternal stakes. But the same Jesus who warns of hell also eats with sinners, weeps over Jerusalem, forgives the guilty, and dies to save His enemies.

Hell shows what sin deserves. The cross shows how far Jesus goes to rescue sinners from it.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Fear Him who can destroy both soul and body in hell.” Matthew 10:28
“The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.” Luke 19:10
“God did not send His Son into the world to condemn the world, but in order that the world might be saved through Him.” John 3:17

questions.question: Does the Old Testament command revenge while Jesus forbids it?
questions.answer:
The Old Testament does not command personal revenge. “Eye for eye” was a principle for proportional public justice, not permission for private vengeance.

Jesus corrects the misuse of that law as a personal retaliation code. In its original setting, “eye for eye” restrained excessive punishment by requiring justice to be proportionate. The Old Testament itself commands love for neighbor and forbids personal vengeance. Jesus deepens and applies that ethic by calling His disciples to refuse retaliation and love enemies.

The movement is not from revenge to love, but from legal proportional justice to kingdom mercy in personal relationships.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Eye for eye, tooth for tooth.” Exodus 21:24
“You shall not take vengeance... but you shall love your neighbor as yourself.” Leviticus 19:18
“Do not resist the one who is evil... Love your enemies.” Matthew 5:39, 44

questions.question: Does the Bible permit polygamy while teaching one-man-one-woman marriage?
questions.answer:
The Bible records polygamy, but it does not present polygamy as God’s creation design. The original pattern is one man and one woman joined as one flesh.

When polygamy appears in Scripture, it regularly brings rivalry, pain, jealousy, and family disorder. God tolerated many practices in a fallen world without endorsing them as ideal. Jesus points back to Genesis, not to the polygamous examples, when teaching marriage. Church leaders are also described as the husband of one wife.

The Bible’s trajectory is clear: marriage is a covenant union of one man and one woman, fulfilled as a picture of Christ and the church.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“A man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife, and they shall become one flesh.” Genesis 2:24
“From the beginning it was not so.” Matthew 19:8
“The husband of one wife.” 1 Timothy 3:2

questions.question: Does the Bible approve incest because Abraham married Sarah?
questions.answer:
The Bible does not approve incest as morally ideal because Abraham married Sarah. Genesis records an early patriarchal situation before later explicit restrictions in the Mosaic law.

Abraham’s marriage to Sarah reflects an early patriarchal setting before Israel received explicit covenant laws about forbidden relations. In Genesis, the human family is still close to its origins, kinship structures are different, and the later Mosaic boundaries have not yet been given. By Leviticus, God clearly prohibits sexual relations with close relatives, protecting family order, covenant holiness, and vulnerable people from exploitation. Recording Abraham’s family situation is not the same as commanding it for all time.

The Bible often records the lives of flawed people within unfolding redemptive history. God’s purposes move forward by grace, not because the patriarchs were morally perfect.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“She is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father though not the daughter of my mother, and she became my wife.” Genesis 20:12
“None of you shall approach any one of his close relatives to uncover nakedness.” Leviticus 18:6
“The times of ignorance God overlooked, but now He commands all people everywhere to repent.” Acts 17:30

questions.question: Does God punish David's child for David's sin, contradicting Ezekiel 18?
questions.answer:
David’s child dying after David’s sin does not mean the child was personally guilty of David’s sin. It shows that a ruler’s sin can bring devastating consequences on others.

Ezekiel 18 addresses personal guilt before God: a son is not condemned as guilty for his father’s sin. David’s child’s death is a temporal judgment within a tragic royal situation, not a statement that the baby bore David’s moral guilt. Sin often harms the innocent in this fallen world, especially when leaders sin. That is part of why sin is so serious.

The passage is painful, but it does not contradict Ezekiel. It distinguishes personal guilt from real-world consequences of another person’s evil.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The child who is born to you shall die.” 2 Samuel 12:14
“The son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father.” Ezekiel 18:20
“Against the LORD, and against His anointed.” 2 Samuel 12:14

questions.question: Does God command love while ordering judgment on Canaan?
questions.answer:
God’s command to love and His judgment on Canaan are not contradictions because love does not cancel justice. The Canaanite judgment was a unique act of divine judgment after long patience, not permission for personal hatred or ethnic violence.

Genesis says the sin of the Amorites was not yet complete in Abraham’s day, showing God delayed judgment for centuries. The conquest is connected to severe wickedness, idolatry, and child sacrifice. Israel was also warned that if they committed the same sins, they too would be judged and expelled from the land. This was not racial superiority; it was holy judgment.

Christians must not use Canaan as a model for religious violence. Jesus commands His church to make disciples by witness, not conquest.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete.” Genesis 15:16
“Because of these abominations the LORD your God is driving them out.” Deuteronomy 18:12
“My kingdom is not of this world... My servants would have been fighting.” John 18:36

questions.question: Does Rahab's lie contradict God's command not to lie?
questions.answer:
Rahab’s lie does not make lying morally good. Scripture praises her faith in protecting God’s people, not the falsehood itself as an ideal moral act.

Rahab lived in a violent pagan city and acted with limited knowledge but real faith in Israel’s God. The Bible commends her for receiving the spies and siding with the LORD against Jericho. It does not pause to approve every method she used. The narrative highlights her courageous allegiance, while the wider Bible continues to condemn lying.

The point is not that deceit becomes righteous. The point is that God showed mercy to a Gentile woman who trusted Him and acted to protect His people.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I know that the LORD has given you the land.” Joshua 2:9
“Rahab the prostitute did not perish... because she had given a friendly welcome to the spies.” Hebrews 11:31
“Having put away falsehood, let each one of you speak the truth.” Ephesians 4:25

questions.question: How do Christians explain the Bible’s teaching on whether God accepts foreigners?
questions.answer:
The Bible consistently shows that God accepts foreigners who turn to Him in faith, while also judging covenant rebellion and idolatry. The issue is not ethnicity but allegiance to the LORD.

Israel was chosen for a mission, not because other nations were worthless. Foreigners such as Rahab, Ruth, Naaman, and the repentant Ninevites receive mercy. The law also commanded care for the sojourner. At the same time, Israel was warned not to adopt idolatry from the nations. In Christ, the promised blessing to the nations becomes explicit and worldwide.

God’s plan was never ethnic contempt. It was covenant holiness leading to blessing for all nations through Messiah. In Christ, Paul can say there is neither Jew nor Greek as a basis for spiritual superiority; all who belong to Christ are one in Him.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The foreigner who joins himself to the LORD... these I will bring to My holy mountain.” Isaiah 56:6-7
“Your people shall be my people, and your God my God.” Ruth 1:16
“In every nation anyone who fears Him and does what is right is acceptable to Him.” Acts 10:35
“There is neither Jew nor Greek... for you are all one in Christ Jesus.” Galatians 3:28

questions.question: Does Matthew misattribute a prophecy to Jeremiah that sounds like Zechariah?
questions.answer:
Matthew is not carelessly confusing Jeremiah and Zechariah. He is drawing together a prophetic pattern that includes details from Zechariah and themes from Jeremiah.

Zechariah 11 contains the thirty pieces of silver and the potter connection. Jeremiah contributes the broader themes of the potter, a purchased field, bloodguilt, and judgment associated with the valley of slaughter. Matthew names Jeremiah because Jeremiah supplies the dominant judgment-and-field framework, while Zechariah supplies the specific betrayal-money wording.

This kind of composite citation was acceptable in Jewish interpretation. Matthew’s point is that Judas’ betrayal money, the potter’s field, and the field of blood are not random details; they fit the prophetic pattern of rejected shepherd, bloodguilt, and coming judgment.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“They weighed out as my wages thirty pieces of silver.” Zechariah 11:12
“Arise, and go down to the potter’s house.” Jeremiah 18:2
“Then was fulfilled what had been spoken by the prophet Jeremiah.” Matthew 27:9

questions.question: Does Matthew misquote Hosea, 'Out of Egypt I called my son'?
questions.answer:
Matthew does not misquote Hosea. Hosea speaks of Israel as God’s son coming out of Egypt, and Matthew presents Jesus as the true Son who fulfills Israel’s story.

This is typology, not a simple one-line prediction. Israel came out of Egypt, passed through water, was tested in the wilderness, and failed. Jesus comes out of Egypt, passes through baptism, is tested in the wilderness, and remains faithful. Matthew is showing that Jesus embodies faithful Israel and brings Israel’s calling to fulfillment.

Hosea’s history becomes prophecy in the fuller biblical sense because God’s earlier acts form patterns completed in Messiah.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Out of Egypt I called My son.” Hosea 11:1
“This was to fulfill what the Lord had spoken by the prophet, ‘Out of Egypt I called My son.’” Matthew 2:15
“This is My beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.” Matthew 3:17

questions.question: Does Matthew invent 'He shall be called a Nazarene'?
questions.answer:
Matthew is not inventing a quotation with “He shall be called a Nazarene.” He says this fulfills what was spoken by the prophets, plural, which points to a prophetic theme rather than one isolated verse.

Nazareth was associated with lowliness and contempt; Nathanael’s “Can anything good come out of Nazareth?” shows the kind of reputation in view. The prophets repeatedly say the servant of the LORD would be despised and rejected. Jesus being known as a Nazarene fits that pattern of a humble, rejected Messiah. There may also be a supporting wordplay with Isaiah’s “branch” language, since the Hebrew branch term netzer sounds like Nazareth, but that should support rather than replace the broader prophetic-theme answer.

The strongest answer is that Matthew is summarizing a prophetic theme: God’s Messiah would not arrive with worldly prestige, but in humility and rejection before glory.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He shall be called a Nazarene.” Matthew 2:23
“He was despised and rejected by men.” Isaiah 53:3
“There shall come forth a shoot from the stump of Jesse.” Isaiah 11:1

questions.question: Does Mark wrongly quote Malachi as Isaiah?
questions.answer:
Mark is not wrongly quoting Malachi as Isaiah. He is combining messenger language from Exodus/Malachi with wilderness language from Isaiah, and Isaiah provides the dominant frame for the opening of the Gospel.

The quotation begins with the promised messenger who prepares the way, echoing Exodus and Malachi. It then moves into Isaiah 40: the voice in the wilderness preparing the way of the LORD. Mark names Isaiah because Isaiah’s new-exodus hope controls the whole introduction: God is coming to rescue His people, and John the Baptist is the wilderness forerunner.

This is a composite citation, not a mistake. Mark’s point is profound: the LORD’s promised way is being prepared, and Jesus steps into the role that reveals God’s saving arrival.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Behold, I send My messenger before your face.” Exodus 23:20
“Behold, I send My messenger, and he will prepare the way before Me.” Malachi 3:1
“The voice of one crying in the wilderness: ‘Prepare the way of the LORD.’” Isaiah 40:3

questions.question: Does Stephen contradict Genesis about Abraham's burial place?
questions.answer:
Stephen’s statement is best understood as a compressed summary of patriarchal burial traditions, not a careful land-title footnote meant to list every purchase separately.

Genesis says Abraham bought the cave of Machpelah from Ephron, where Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah were buried. Genesis also says Jacob bought land near Shechem from the sons of Hamor. Stephen rapidly summarizes Israel’s history in a speech, combining memories of patriarchal burials and land purchases while moving toward his main point: Israel repeatedly resisted God’s messengers.

Ancient speeches often compressed historical details that the audience already knew. The difficulty is real, but it is not a contradiction that affects Stephen’s argument. His point is theological: God was faithful to the patriarchs, and Israel’s leaders were resisting the Righteous One just as earlier generations resisted God’s servants.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Abraham weighed out... silver... for the field and the cave.” Genesis 23:16-17
“Jacob bought the piece of land... from the sons of Hamor.” Genesis 33:19
“You always resist the Holy Spirit.” Acts 7:51

questions.question: Did Jacob's family entering Egypt number seventy or seventy-five?
questions.answer:
The difference between seventy and seventy-five comes from different ways of counting Jacob’s family and from the textual tradition used in Acts.

Genesis in the Hebrew text counts seventy persons connected with Jacob entering Egypt. The Greek Old Testament tradition includes additional descendants, giving seventy-five. Stephen, speaking in Acts, follows that Greek textual tradition, which was commonly used in the first-century Jewish world. The difference is not a contradiction about the event but a difference in counting and textual tradition.

Both numbers communicate the same theological point: God brought Jacob’s family into Egypt and later multiplied them into the nation He redeemed.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“All the persons of the house of Jacob who came into Egypt were seventy.” Genesis 46:27
“Joseph sent and summoned Jacob his father and all his kindred, seventy-five persons in all.” Acts 7:14
“The people of Israel were fruitful and increased greatly.” Exodus 1:7

questions.question: Does Acts contradict Galatians about Paul's Jerusalem visits?
questions.answer:
Acts and Galatians do not contradict each other about Paul’s Jerusalem visits. They select different visits and emphasize different purposes.

Galatians is not trying to list every trip Paul ever made to Jerusalem. Paul is defending that his Gospel did not come from human authorization, so he highlights visits relevant to that argument. Acts gives a broader historical narrative of the church’s growth and includes visits connected with conversion, famine relief, and the Jerusalem council. Different selectivity is not contradiction.

The accounts agree on the central point: Paul’s Gospel came from Christ, and the Jerusalem apostles ultimately recognized the grace given to him.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I did not immediately consult with anyone... nor did I go up to Jerusalem.” Galatians 1:16-17
“Then after three years I went up to Jerusalem.” Galatians 1:18
“They gave the right hand of fellowship to Barnabas and me.” Galatians 2:9

questions.question: Does Mark 2 make Abiathar high priest when 1 Samuel names Ahimelech?
questions.answer:
Mark’s phrase can mean “in the section” or “in the period associated with Abiathar the high priest,” not that Abiathar personally handed David the bread.

First Samuel names Ahimelech as the priest who gave David the consecrated bread. Abiathar was Ahimelech’s son, the famous survivor of Saul’s massacre of the priests, and the priest closely associated with David afterward. Naming Abiathar locates the event in the priestly period connected with the best-known figure from that episode’s aftermath.

Jesus’ argument does not depend on confusing the two men. His point is that David’s need showed ceremonial regulations were never meant to crush mercy, and that the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The priest gave him the holy bread.” 1 Samuel 21:6
“In the time of Abiathar the high priest.” Mark 2:26
“The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath.” Mark 2:27

questions.question: Did Jesus say mustard seed is the smallest seed, though other seeds are smaller?
questions.answer:
Jesus was not giving a botanical catalog of every seed on earth. He was using a familiar agricultural comparison from His audience’s world.

In Jewish teaching, the mustard seed was commonly used as an image of something extremely small. In the garden context Jesus refers to, it was among the smallest seeds people planted, yet it grew into a surprisingly large plant. The point of the parable is not botany trivia but kingdom contrast: God’s kingdom begins in apparent smallness and grows beyond human expectation.

Jesus’ teaching is accurate to the ordinary language and setting of His hearers. The parable’s truth is about the kingdom’s hidden power.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“It is the smallest of all seeds, but when it has grown it is larger than all the garden plants.” Matthew 13:32
“The kingdom of heaven is like a grain of mustard seed.” Matthew 13:31
“God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong.” 1 Corinthians 1:27

questions.question: Does the Bible say bats are birds?
questions.answer:
The Bible is not classifying bats according to modern biological taxonomy. Leviticus groups creatures by ordinary ancient categories of movement and habitat.

In the clean and unclean food laws, flying creatures are listed together from the perspective of how Israelites encountered them. A bat is a flying creature, so it appears in that practical category. Modern taxonomy classifies bats as mammals, but Leviticus is not trying to teach modern zoological categories. It is giving covenant food distinctions in ordinary observational language.

There is no scientific contradiction because the text is not making the kind of technical claim modern biology makes.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“And these you shall detest among the birds... the bat.” Leviticus 11:13, 19
“You shall distinguish between the clean and the unclean.” Leviticus 11:47
“All Scripture is breathed out by God.” 2 Timothy 3:16

questions.question: Does the Bible say insects have four legs?
questions.answer:
Leviticus is not saying insects have only four legs in modern anatomical terms. It is using ordinary observational language for winged insects that move on four main walking legs while having additional jointed legs used for leaping.

The passage itself mentions “jointed legs above their feet” for hopping insects such as locusts. That shows the writer is aware of the additional limbs. The point is not an anatomy lecture but distinguishing which swarming creatures were clean or unclean for Israel. Ancient practical categories should not be judged as if they were written in the language of a modern biology textbook.

The text is coherent on its own terms: it describes how these creatures move and which were permitted as food under the Mosaic law.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“All winged insects that go on all fours are detestable to you.” Leviticus 11:20
“Those that have jointed legs above their feet, with which to hop on the ground.” Leviticus 11:21
“These you may eat: the locust of any kind.” Leviticus 11:22

questions.question: Does the Bible teach a flat earth?
questions.answer:
The Bible does not teach a flat earth as doctrine. It often uses ordinary observational and poetic language, the same way people today say “sunrise” without denying the earth’s rotation.

Expressions like “the ends of the earth” or “four corners of the earth” are figures of speech for the whole world, not geometry lessons. Biblical poetry describes creation in vivid imagery because it is teaching God’s sovereignty, not giving a technical map. When Scripture speaks theologically, it is clear: God created, rules, sustains, and judges the whole earth.

Reading poetic and phenomenological language as scientific error misunderstands how language works. The Bible speaks truly, but not always in modern technical categories.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He sits above the circle of the earth.” Isaiah 40:22
“He... gathers the dispersed of Judah from the four corners of the earth.” Isaiah 11:12
“The earth is the LORD’s and the fullness thereof.” Psalm 24:1

questions.question: Does Joshua saying the sun stood still prove scientific error?
questions.answer:
Joshua’s account uses ordinary observational language: from the perspective of people on earth, the sun stood still. We still speak that way when we say the sun rises or sets.

The passage is not trying to explain celestial mechanics. It reports an extraordinary act of God giving Israel victory. Whether God lengthened the day by affecting the earth’s rotation, atmospheric light, or another means, the theological claim is that the Creator ruled creation for His redemptive purpose. The language is from human observation because that is how people naturally describe daylight.

The miracle is not a scientific blunder. It is a claim that the God who made the heavens can govern them.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Sun, stand still at Gibeon.” Joshua 10:12
“The sun stopped in the midst of heaven.” Joshua 10:13
“Is anything too hard for the LORD?” Genesis 18:14

questions.question: How do Christians explain the Bible’s teaching on how many animals entered Noah's ark?
questions.answer:
Genesis 6 gives the general rule; Genesis 7 gives the expanded instruction for clean animals. There is no contradiction.

Two of every kind were brought to preserve life. Clean animals were brought in greater numbers because they would be needed for sacrifice after the flood and for future multiplication. The later detail does not cancel the earlier summary; it adds an important distinction within it.

The point is that God preserved animal life through judgment and provided what was needed for worship and renewal after the flood.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Two of every sort shall come in to you to keep them alive.” Genesis 6:20
“Take with you seven pairs of all clean animals... and a pair of the animals that are not clean.” Genesis 7:2
“Noah built an altar to the LORD.” Genesis 8:20

questions.question: Did the flood last forty days or much longer?
questions.answer:
The flood rain lasted forty days, but the whole flood event lasted much longer. Those are different time references, not conflicting durations.

Genesis says rain fell for forty days and forty nights. It also says the waters prevailed on the earth for 150 days and then gradually receded. Noah remained in the ark while the waters rose, prevailed, decreased, and the earth dried. The forty days describes the period of intense rainfall; the longer timeline describes the entire flood and recovery period.

The narrative is careful with time markers. It distinguishes the storm itself from the full duration of judgment and deliverance.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Rain fell upon the earth forty days and forty nights.” Genesis 7:12
“The waters prevailed on the earth 150 days.” Genesis 7:24
“In the second month... the earth had dried out.” Genesis 8:14

questions.question: Did Noah send a raven or a dove?
questions.answer:
Noah sent both a raven and a dove. Genesis describes a sequence of actions, not competing versions.

The raven was sent first and went back and forth until the waters dried. Then Noah sent a dove to test whether the waters had receded from the ground. He sent the dove more than once, receiving an olive leaf and finally seeing that it did not return. Each bird served a different role in the unfolding account.

The details show Noah patiently waiting for God’s timing rather than rushing out of the ark on his own.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He sent forth a raven.” Genesis 8:7
“Then he sent forth a dove from him.” Genesis 8:8
“The dove came back to him in the evening, and behold, in her mouth was a freshly plucked olive leaf.” Genesis 8:11

questions.question: How do Christians explain the Bible’s teaching on who wrote the Ten Commandments?
questions.answer:
God wrote the Ten Words on the tablets. Moses wrote the broader covenant words and instructions connected with the covenant renewal.

Exodus 31 says the first tablets were written by the finger of God. After Moses broke them, God commanded Moses to cut new tablets and said He would write on them the words that were on the first tablets. In Exodus 34, when it says “he wrote,” the subject can refer back to the LORD, or the statement can refer to Moses recording the broader covenant words. Either way, the Ten Commandments themselves come from God.

The account emphasizes both truths: God is the divine author of the covenant commands, and Moses is the mediator who receives and records God’s covenant instruction for Israel.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Tablets of stone, written with the finger of God.” Exodus 31:18
“I will write on the tablets the words that were on the first tablets.” Exodus 34:1
“He declared to you His covenant... the Ten Commandments.” Deuteronomy 4:13

questions.question: Did Moses speak well or say he was slow of speech?
questions.answer:
Moses could be “slow of speech” and still later speak with authority because eloquence and divine calling are not the same thing.

In Exodus 4, Moses says he is not eloquent and lacks natural confidence. God does not deny Moses’ weakness; He promises to be with his mouth and gives Aaron to help as spokesman. That does not mean Moses never spoke. Later, Moses speaks powerfully because God authorizes him, gives him His words, and forms him through the calling.

There is no contradiction. Moses’ weakness magnifies God’s power: the deliverance of Israel rests on the LORD’s word, not on Moses’ natural rhetorical skill.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I am slow of speech and slow of tongue.” Exodus 4:10
“I will be with your mouth and teach you what you shall speak.” Exodus 4:12
“Moses spoke all the words of this song.” Deuteronomy 31:30

questions.question: Did all of Saul's household die or did descendants remain?
questions.answer:
Statements about all of Saul’s household dying are not absolute claims that no descendant remained. They describe the collapse of Saul’s royal house in a particular battle and political line.

Saul and several sons died at Mount Gilboa, ending his kingship in judgment. Yet other descendants remained, including Ish-bosheth and Mephibosheth. Scripture itself records them, so it is not unaware of survivors. “All his house” can refer to the fighting household present with him or the effective fall of his dynasty, not every biological descendant without exception.

The point is that Saul’s royal house fell under judgment, while God later showed covenant mercy to Mephibosheth through David.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Thus Saul died; he and his three sons and all his house died together.” 1 Chronicles 10:6
“Ish-bosheth, Saul’s son, was forty years old when he began to reign.” 2 Samuel 2:10
“Mephibosheth... shall eat at my table always.” 2 Samuel 9:10

questions.question: How do Christians explain the Bible’s teaching on whether Michal had children?
questions.answer:
The strongest explanation is that 2 Samuel 21:8 should be read as Merab, not Michal, because Merab was the daughter of Saul who married Adriel and bore children connected to him.

Second Samuel 6 says Michal had no child to the day of her death. First Samuel says Merab, Saul’s daughter, was given to Adriel. That makes Merab the fitting mother of the sons in 2 Samuel 21. Some manuscripts and translations reflect this reading. The mention of Michal is therefore best understood as a textual transmission problem, not a claim that Michal bore children after being declared childless.

A secondary possibility is that Michal raised or cared for children connected to Merab, but the clearest solution is the Merab reading. Either way, Scripture’s own family details show Michal herself did not bear those sons.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Michal the daughter of Saul had no child to the day of her death.” 2 Samuel 6:23
“Merab... was given to Adriel the Meholathite for a wife.” 1 Samuel 18:19
“The five sons of Merab the daughter of Saul, whom she bore to Adriel.” 2 Samuel 21:8

questions.question: Did Solomon have 550 officials or 250 officials?
questions.answer:
The 550 and 250 figures refer to different categories of Solomon’s officials, not the same group counted inconsistently.

Kings and Chronicles both distinguish between ordinary overseers and higher-ranking chief officers. In Kings, 3,300 plus 550 gives 3,850 total officials connected to the work. In Chronicles, 3,600 plus 250 also gives 3,850. The totals correspond when the administrative categories are separated differently. One writer places more men in the lower overseer category and the other in the higher category, but the combined administrative total matches.

So this is not a contradiction. It is a difference in classification within Solomon’s large building administration.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Besides Solomon’s 3,300 chief officers.” 1 Kings 5:16
“These were the chief officers... 550.” 1 Kings 9:23
“There were 250 chief officers.” 2 Chronicles 8:10

questions.question: Did Jesus heal one blind man near Jericho or two?
questions.answer:
Jesus healed two blind men near Jericho, and Mark highlights Bartimaeus, the named man. Mentioning one healed man does not deny that another was healed.

Matthew gives the fuller number: two blind men. Mark focuses on Bartimaeus by name and gives a vivid personal account. Luke also focuses on one man. This is normal reporting. If two people are healed and one is the main speaker or became known in the community, an author may focus on him without claiming he was the only person present.

The accounts agree that Jesus opened blind eyes as the Son of David, showing messianic compassion and power.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“There were two blind men sitting by the roadside.” Matthew 20:30
“Bartimaeus, a blind beggar... was sitting by the roadside.” Mark 10:46
“Lord, let our eyes be opened.” Matthew 20:33

questions.question: Was the blind man healed as Jesus entered or left Jericho?
questions.answer:
The blind man was healed near Jericho as Jesus traveled through the Jericho area. The difference between “entering” and “leaving” is explained by the existence of old Jericho and Herodian/Roman Jericho near each other.

A traveler could be leaving the old city area while approaching the newer city, or leaving the broader Jericho region while another writer describes the approach to a specific part of it. Matthew and Mark emphasize Jesus leaving Jericho; Luke says He drew near. The geography allows both descriptions to be true in the same journey.

The accounts agree on the substance: near Jericho, a blind man cried out to Jesus as Son of David, and Jesus mercifully restored his sight.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“As they went out of Jericho, a great crowd followed Him.” Matthew 20:29
“As He drew near to Jericho, a blind man was sitting by the roadside.” Luke 18:35
“Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!” Luke 18:38

questions.question: Did Jesus heal one demoniac or two in the region of the Gadarenes?
questions.answer:
Jesus healed two demoniacs, and Mark and Luke focus on the one who was most prominent, violent, or later became the main witness.

Matthew mentions two demon-possessed men. Mark and Luke describe one in detail, including his isolation, chains, torment, and restoration. Focusing on one person does not deny that another was there. Writers often highlight the main speaker or central figure in an event. The core details match: Jesus confronts demons, sends them into pigs, and the people of the region react with fear.

The accounts agree that Jesus has authority over the demonic realm and restores ruined people.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Two demon-possessed men met Him.” Matthew 8:28
“There met Him out of the tombs a man with an unclean spirit.” Mark 5:2
“Go home to your friends and tell them how much the Lord has done for you.” Mark 5:19

questions.question: Were the demons sent into pigs in Gerasa, Gadara, or Gergesa?
questions.answer:
Gerasa, Gadara, and Gergesa can refer to the same general Gentile region from different geographic angles or textual traditions.

Gadara was a significant city whose territory could extend toward the Sea of Galilee. Gerasa can refer to a wider district in some textual traditions. Gergesa is associated with a lakeside location that fits the steep bank detail. Ancient place names often identified a city, region, or district rather than only a modern point on a map.

The accounts agree on the event itself: east of the lake in Gentile territory, Jesus delivered a demonized man, the demons entered pigs, and the herd rushed down a steep bank into the water. The geography variation does not change the miracle or its meaning.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“The country of the Gadarenes.” Matthew 8:28
“The country of the Gerasenes.” Mark 5:1
“The herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea.” Mark 5:13

questions.question: Did Jairus say his daughter had died or was dying?
questions.answer:
Jairus’ daughter was at the point of death when he first sought Jesus, and by the time Jesus was on the way, news arrived that she had died. The accounts compress the timeline differently.

Mark and Luke give the fuller sequence: Jairus says she is dying, then messengers report her death. Matthew summarizes the situation from the perspective of the completed crisis: the daughter has died. Ancient narration often compresses events to their final outcome. There is no conflict in saying Jairus came in desperation as death approached and that the situation became death before Jesus arrived.

The miracle’s point is the same: Jesus has authority not only over sickness but over death itself.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“My little daughter is at the point of death.” Mark 5:23
“Your daughter is dead. Why trouble the Teacher any further?” Mark 5:35
“My daughter has just died, but come and lay Your hand on her.” Matthew 9:18

questions.question: Did Jesus heal the centurion's servant directly or through messengers?
questions.answer:
Jesus healed the centurion’s servant through the centurion’s authorized messengers, and Matthew summarizes the encounter as if the centurion came himself.

That was normal representative language. If an ambassador speaks for a king, the king can be said to have made the request. Luke gives the detailed version: Jewish elders and friends speak on the centurion’s behalf. Matthew gives the compressed version, focusing on the centurion’s faith and Jesus’ authority.

Both accounts agree on the point: the centurion understood authority so well that he knew Jesus did not need to enter the house. One word from Christ was enough to heal.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“A centurion came forward to Him.” Matthew 8:5
“The centurion sent to Him elders of the Jews.” Luke 7:3
“Only say the word, and my servant will be healed.” Matthew 8:8

questions.question: Did the leper ask Jesus for healing before or after the Sermon on the Mount?
questions.answer:
The leper’s healing is placed differently because Matthew arranges some material thematically after the Sermon on the Mount, while Mark and Luke give a more chronological flow.

Matthew groups miracles after Jesus’ teaching to show the authority of the kingdom in action: Jesus teaches with authority, then cleanses, heals, and delivers. Mark and Luke place the leper earlier in their narrative sequence. Ancient biography allowed thematic arrangement, especially when the author was making a theological point. The event itself is the same: Jesus touches and cleanses a leper.

The placement does not change the truth of the miracle. Jesus has authority to make the unclean clean.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“When He came down from the mountain... a leper came to Him.” Matthew 8:1-2
“A leper came to Him, imploring Him.” Mark 1:40
“I will; be clean.” Mark 1:41

questions.question: Did Jesus feed four thousand or five thousand?
questions.answer:
Jesus fed five thousand and also fed four thousand. The Gospels present them as two separate miracles, not conflicting numbers for one event.

The details differ: different crowd sizes, different amounts of bread and fish, different leftovers, and different locations. Jesus Himself later refers to both feedings separately, asking the disciples to remember the five thousand and the four thousand. That settles the issue within the Gospel narrative.

The repeated miracles show Jesus’ abundant provision for Israel and the nations, revealing Him as the bread-giving Messiah.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Those who ate were about five thousand men.” Mark 6:44
“Those who ate were four thousand men.” Mark 8:9
“When I broke the five loaves for the five thousand... and the seven for the four thousand...” Mark 8:19-20

questions.question: Did Peter confess Jesus before or after the feeding events?
questions.answer:
Peter’s confession comes after the major feeding events in Matthew and Mark, and Luke’s shorter narrative arrangement does not contradict that sequence.

The Gospels are selective. Luke omits the feeding of the four thousand and moves from the feeding of the five thousand toward Peter’s confession. Matthew and Mark include additional events before the confession at Caesarea Philippi. The difference is not that Peter confessed at incompatible times, but that Luke has chosen a tighter narrative path.

The theological climax is shared: Peter confesses Jesus as the Christ, and Jesus begins to teach plainly about His suffering.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.” Matthew 16:16
“You are the Christ.” Mark 8:29
“The Christ of God.” Luke 9:20

questions.question: Did the Transfiguration happen after six days or about eight days?
questions.answer:
The Transfiguration happened after six days, or about eight days, depending on how the days are counted. Both expressions refer to the same interval.

Matthew and Mark count the six full days between Jesus’ saying and the Transfiguration. Luke says “about eight days,” which can include the day Jesus spoke, the six intervening days, and the day of the Transfiguration. Luke also signals approximation with “about.” There is no contradiction between precise middle days and inclusive counting.

The accounts agree on the event’s meaning: Jesus is revealed in glory as the beloved Son, superior to Moses and Elijah.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“After six days Jesus took with Him Peter and James, and John.” Matthew 17:1
“Now about eight days after these sayings He took with Him Peter and John and James.” Luke 9:28
“This is My beloved Son... listen to Him.” Matthew 17:5

questions.question: Did Jesus curse the fig tree before or after cleansing the temple?
questions.answer:
Matthew compresses the fig tree episode, while Mark spreads it over two days around the temple cleansing. The event is the same, but Matthew presents it more topically.

Mark gives the sequence: Jesus curses the fig tree, cleanses the temple, then the disciples see the withered tree the next morning. Matthew condenses the action and result, focusing on the lesson about judgment and faith. Ancient narrative often compresses time when the author’s emphasis is theological rather than chronological detail.

The fig tree and temple cleansing belong together: both symbolize judgment on fruitless religion.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He found nothing on it but leaves.” Mark 11:13
“They saw the fig tree withered away to its roots.” Mark 11:20
“May no fruit ever come from you again!” Matthew 21:19

questions.question: Did Jesus ride one donkey or two at the triumphal entry?
questions.answer:
Jesus rode the colt. Matthew mentions two animals because the mother donkey and her colt were both present, while Mark, Luke, and John focus on the animal Jesus actually rode.

The pressure point is Matthew 21:7: “He sat on them.” In ordinary Greek, the nearest plural can refer to the cloaks just mentioned, not to Jesus straddling two animals. The disciples put cloaks on the animals, and Jesus sat on the cloaks placed on the colt. Matthew is not picturing an impossible two-animal ride.

Matthew also has a reason to mention both animals. Zechariah 9:9 uses Hebrew poetic parallelism: “mounted on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.” That is not two separate mounts, but two parallel descriptions of the promised King’s humble arrival. Matthew names the mother and colt to show the full scene of fulfillment; the other Gospel writers simply report the ridden colt.

The accounts agree on the main fact: Jesus entered Jerusalem as the humble Davidic King promised by the prophet.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Humble and mounted on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.” Zechariah 9:9
“They brought the donkey and the colt and put on them their cloaks, and He sat on them.” Matthew 21:7
“They brought it to Jesus... and He sat on it.” Mark 11:7

questions.question: Did the crowd shout different words at the triumphal entry?
questions.answer:
The crowd’s words at the triumphal entry differ slightly because the Gospel writers preserve different parts of the same public shouting.

A crowd does not chant only one sentence in perfect unison. People cry out multiple phrases: “Hosanna,” “Blessed is He who comes,” “King of Israel,” “peace in heaven,” and “Son of David.” Each Gospel selects wording that fits its emphasis. The differences are complementary reports of a lively public event, not contradictory transcripts.

The shared message is clear: Jesus enters Jerusalem as the promised King, and the crowd’s praise echoes Psalm 118.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Hosanna to the Son of David!” Matthew 21:9
“Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord!” Psalm 118:26
“Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord!” Luke 19:38

questions.question: Did Jesus answer the high priest or remain silent?
questions.answer:
Jesus both remained silent before false accusations and answered when the high priest directly charged Him under solemn questioning about His identity.

The Gospels say Jesus did not answer the many accusations brought against Him. That silence fulfills the pattern of the suffering servant who does not defend Himself against unjust charges. But when asked directly whether He is the Christ, the Son of the Blessed, Jesus answers truthfully and identifies Himself with the Son of Man who will come in glory.

There is no contradiction. Jesus is silent before slander, but He confesses the truth about who He is.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“He gave him no answer, not even to a single charge.” Matthew 27:14
“Are You the Christ, the Son of the Blessed? And Jesus said, ‘I am.’” Mark 14:61-62
“Like a lamb that is led to the slaughter... He opened not His mouth.” Isaiah 53:7

questions.question: Did Pilate find Jesus innocent but still crucify Him?
questions.answer:
Pilate found no guilt in Jesus and still handed Him over because he was politically weak, feared the crowd, and chose expediency over justice.

That is not a contradiction; it is the moral failure at the heart of the trial. Pilate repeatedly recognizes that Jesus has not committed a capital crime, yet the leaders pressure him by accusing Jesus of rival kingship and threatening Pilate’s loyalty to Caesar. Pilate tries to avoid responsibility, but washing his hands does not make injustice innocent.

The Gospels show Jesus condemned by human cowardice and political compromise, while God uses even that injustice to accomplish redemption.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“I find no guilt in this man.” Luke 23:4
“He delivered Him over to them to be crucified.” John 19:16
“This Jesus... delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified.” Acts 2:23

questions.question: Did Jesus' resurrection body have wounds but also pass through locked doors?
questions.answer:
Jesus’ resurrection body was physical and glorified. The wounds show continuity with the crucified body; passing through locked doors shows His body is transformed and no longer limited in the ordinary way.

The resurrection is not a return to mortal life like Lazarus. Jesus rises bodily, can be touched, eats food, and bears wounds. Yet He also appears in locked rooms and is no longer subject to death. This is not contradiction but glorification. The same Jesus who died is alive forever in a body fitted for the new creation.

Christian hope follows His pattern: not escape from the body, but bodily resurrection transformed by God’s power.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“See My hands and My feet, that it is I Myself.” Luke 24:39
“Although the doors were locked, Jesus came and stood among them.” John 20:26
“It is raised a spiritual body.” 1 Corinthians 15:44

questions.question: Did Jesus tell Mary not to touch Him, but tell Thomas to touch Him?
questions.answer:
Jesus’ words to Mary do not mean she was forbidden to touch Him in every sense. They mean she must not cling to Him as though His relationship with the disciples would continue exactly as before.

Mary encounters the risen Jesus and wants to hold on. Jesus redirects her: He is ascending to the Father and sending her to announce the news. Thomas, by contrast, needs to stop doubting and believe, so Jesus invites him to touch His wounds. The two situations are different: Mary must not cling to the old mode of presence; Thomas must face the physical reality of the resurrection.

Both moments teach the same truth: Jesus is truly risen and is leading His people into a new resurrection relationship with Him.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Do not cling to Me, for I have not yet ascended to the Father.” John 20:17
“Put your finger here, and see My hands.” John 20:27
“Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.” John 20:29

questions.question: Did Matthew say the guards reported the resurrection, while other Gospels omit guards?
questions.answer:
Matthew includes the guards because they serve his purpose of answering the early claim that the disciples stole the body. The other Gospels omit them because they are focusing on other witnesses and events.

Omission is not contradiction. Each Gospel writer selects material. Matthew is especially concerned to show how the authorities responded to Jesus, from Herod’s hostility at His birth to the bribery surrounding the empty tomb. The guards’ report explains why the stolen-body rumor circulated and also confirms that the tomb was empty.

The other Gospels do not deny the guards. They simply do not include that part of the story.

KEY SCRIPTURE
“Pilate said to them, ‘You have a guard of soldiers.’” Matthew 27:65
“Some of the guard went into the city and told the chief priests all that had taken place.” Matthew 28:11
“This story has been spread among the Jews to this day.” Matthew 28:15

questions.question: Did the disciples doubt after seeing the risen Jesus?
questions.answer:
Some disciples doubted after seeing the risen Jesus because the resurrection was overwhelming, unexpected, and required them to understand reality in a new way. Doubt in that moment does not cancel the appearance.

Matthew says some doubted when they saw Him. Luke says the disciples initially disbelieved for joy and were marveling. John tells of Thomas needing direct confirmation.
